toolUseResult.content:
---
name: mcp-phone-controll-testing
description: On-device Flutter testing methodology using the mcp-phone-controll MCP server. Use when planning, executing, or reviewing tests run against a real Android or iOS device through the MCP tools (list_devices, install_app, tap_text, run_integration_tests, screenshot, read_logs, etc.). Provides a Test Validity Rubric to decide which test cases are valid for the requirement and which need to be rewritten before they're worth running, plus a failure-triage cheat sheet keyed to the MCP's Failure codes. Also use when the user asks to verify acceptance criteria on hardware, build a regression suite, or audit an existing test plan against a real device run.
---

# On-Device Testing — mcp-phone-controll

## Purpose

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      WHY THIS SKILL EXISTS                                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│  The MCP gives Claude superpowers on a real phone. Without a    │
│  methodology those superpowers produce noisy, flaky, "ran but   │
│  proves nothing" tests. This skill keeps every on-device run    │
│  tied to:                                                       │
│                                                                 │
│   1. A specific requirement / acceptance criterion              │
│   2. A test case that meets the Validity Rubric                 │
│   3. An MCP tool sequence that produces evidence Claude can     │
│      cite (screenshot path, log line, structured TestRun)       │
│   4. A failure triage path keyed to typed Failure codes         │
│   5. A cross-platform parity check when the feature ships on    │
│      both Android and iOS                                       │
│                                                                 │
│  If a test case can't pass the Rubric, REWRITE IT before        │
│  running. A red test that proves nothing is worse than no test. │
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## When to use

Trigger this skill when the user is:

- planning a test run that will hit a real device through `mcp-phone-controll`
- writing or reviewing `integration_test/` Flutter tests destined for hardware
- auditing an existing test plan ("are these still valid?")
- triaging a failed `run_integration_tests` result
- adding cross-platform coverage (Android-tested feature now needs iOS sign-off)
- building a regression checklist for release sign-off

Skip this skill for:

- Pure unit tests (use the standard `flutter-testing` skill — they don't need a device).
- Widget tests that run in `flutter test` without a device.
- One-off "click around and see" exploration where evidence isn't required.

## The Test Validity Rubric

Every on-device test case must pass **all** of these before it's worth running. If any item is "no", REWRITE the case (or split it).

| # | Criterion             | Concrete check                                                              |
|---|-----------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Mapped to AC**      | The case names the exact acceptance criterion or requirement ID it covers. |
| 2 | **Single behaviour**  | One `Given/When/Then`. Multiple `When`s = split.                            |
| 3 | **Deterministic**     | No reliance on real time, network, randomness, or device-specific defaults. |
| 4 | **Observable via MCP**| The "Then" can be verified by `assert_visible`, `read_logs` regex, or a `TestRun` field — not just "looks right". |
| 5 | **Isolated**          | Runs after `clear_app_data` (or fresh install) — no carry-over from prior tests. |
| 6 | **Idempotent setup**  | Re-running the case from scratch yields identical evidence.                 |
| 7 | **Bounded waits**     | Every wait has a numeric `timeout_s`. No "sleep until it works" fallback.   |
| 8 | **Cross-platform safe** | Uses semantic selectors (`text=`, `resource_id=`) — not raw coordinates that drift across screen sizes. |
| 9 | **Failure-aware**     | The case states what failure mode it would catch (so a regression in that mode actually breaks it). |
| 10| **Cheap diagnosis**   | On failure, captures `screenshot` + `read_logs(min_level="W")` so triage doesn't need a re-run. |

**Rewrite signals** — if you see any of these, the test case is invalid, not the code:

- "Test passes locally, fails in CI" with no further detail → criterion 3 fails.
- Uses `Future.delayed(Duration(seconds: 5))` to wait for UI → criterion 7.
- Asserts `find.byType(Container)` count → criterion 4 (not behaviour, just structure).
- Depends on a previous test having logged in → criterion 5.
- Asserts pixel positions → criterion 8.
- "It's just a smoke test" with no AC mapped → criterion 1.

## Requirement → Test Traceability

Before adding tests, build (or have the user supply) a 3-column matrix:

| AC ID | Acceptance criterion (one sentence) | Test case ID(s) covering it |
|-------|------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| AC-1  | A signed-in user sees their email on the home screen. | TC-HOME-01 |
| AC-2  | Tapping "Sign out" returns to the login screen within 2s. | TC-AUTH-07 |

Rules:

- Every AC needs **≥ 1** test case. If it doesn't, flag the gap, don't invent the test silently.
- Every test case needs **exactly 1** AC. Multi-AC cases are too coarse to localise failures.
- ACs without a clear "Then" observable through MCP are a **specification bug**, not a test problem — flag it.

## Test pyramid for this MCP

```
                        ┌──────────────┐
                        │  smoke (5)   │  ← run on every commit on hardware
                        │  golden flow │
                        └──────────────┘
                     ┌──────────────────┐
                     │ integration (~30)│  ← `run_integration_tests`
                     │  per-feature     │     on hardware before release
                     └──────────────────┘
                ┌──────────────────────────┐
                │     widget (~hundreds)   │  ← `flutter test` — no MCP
                └──────────────────────────┘
            ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
            │       unit (~thousands)          │  ← `flutter test` — no MCP
            └──────────────────────────────────┘
```

Decide where a case belongs:

- **Pure logic / pure mapping** → unit. Don't waste a phone for it.
- **Widget rendering, no I/O** → widget test in-process.
- **Real BLoC + real repos + real platform channel** → integration on hardware.
- **Whole user journey (sign in → action → sign out)** → smoke flow on hardware.

If a unit-level concern is being verified at integration level, that's a **misplaced test** — recommend moving it down. Conversely, if a hardware-only concern (real ATT prompt, real biometric) is being mocked at widget level, recommend moving it up.

## MCP loop patterns

Every on-device test case should follow one of these shapes. Pick the smallest that proves the AC.

### A) Smoke install loop (criteria 1–10)

```
new_session(label="smoke-<sha>")
list_devices                       → confirm at least one device
select_device(serial=...)
install_app(project_path=..., mode="debug")
launch_app(package_id=...)
wait_for_element(text="<known boot text>", timeout_s=15)
take_screenshot(label="post-launch")
read_logs(min_level="W", since_s=10)   → assert no FATAL/E in app's tag
```

If `wait_for_element` times out → the build is broken; do **not** write more cases until this loop is green.

### B) Single-AC integration case

```
clear_app_data(package_id=...)        ← criterion 5
launch_app(package_id=...)
wait_for_element(<entry point>, timeout_s=10)
... drive to the screen under test using tap_text/type_text ...
assert_visible(<expected element>, timeout_s=5)   ← the "Then"
take_screenshot(label="ac-<id>-pass")
```

### C) Negative path

Same as B, but the "Then" is `assert_visible(<error/empty state>)`. Capture both the screenshot AND a `read_logs(tag="<feature>", min_level="W")` so the failure mode is recorded as evidence.

### D) Long-running async ("after X happens, Y appears")

```
tap_text("Submit")
tail_logs(until_pattern="job_completed=true", tag="myapp", timeout_s=30)
assert_visible(<success toast>, timeout_s=5)
```

Never poll `find_element` in a loop yourself — use `wait_for_element` or `tail_logs`.

### E) Suite run (CI-style)

```
new_session(label="release-<sha>")
list_devices
select_device(serial=...)
install_app(project_path=..., mode="profile")
run_integration_tests(project_path=..., test_path="integration_test/")
```

Inspect the returned `TestRun`. Attach failures to AC IDs in your report. If `errored > 0`, treat as infrastructure failure (criterion 3 violation), not a code regression — re-run before filing.

## Failure-triage cheat sheet

Every MCP envelope carries `error.code`. Use it to decide the next move — don't guess.

| Code                        | Most likely cause                                  | Next MCP tool to call                            |
|-----------------------------|----------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| `NoDeviceSelectedFailure`   | Skipped `select_device`.                           | `list_devices` → `select_device`.                |
| `DeviceNotFoundFailure`     | Cable popped, device locked, or never listed.      | `list_devices`. If absent, ask user to check USB. |
| `DeviceOfflineFailure`      | Device authorised this host before but is now off.  | Ask user to unlock + re-tap "Trust".             |
| `AdbFailure`                | Raw adb non-zero exit. Read `details.stderr`.      | If `unauthorized` → re-trust. If `device offline` → reconnect. |
| `BuildFailure`              | `flutter build` failed.                            | Stop. Read `details.stderr`. Don't run more tests until build is green. |
| `InstallFailure`            | APK signature mismatch / older version installed.  | `uninstall_app` then retry `install_app`.        |
| `LaunchFailure` (iOS)       | Developer Disk Image not mounted.                  | Ask user to run `pymobiledevice3 mounter auto-mount`. |
| `UiElementNotFoundFailure`  | Selector wrong, screen changed, or app crashed.    | `take_screenshot` + `dump_ui` + `read_logs`.     |
| `TimeoutFailure`            | Wait was tight, or the trigger never fired.        | Increase `timeout_s` once. If still failing, treat as real bug. |
| `UiFailure`                 | UIA2 / WDA disconnected.                           | Re-`list_devices` (forces reconnect via factory). |
| `TestExecutionFailure`      | `flutter test` produced no JSON events.            | Read `details.stderr`. Often a missing dep or invalid `pubspec`. |
| `FilesystemFailure`         | Artifact dir not writable.                         | `new_session` again or check `~/.mcp_phone_controll/`. |
| `InvalidArgumentFailure`    | Bad/missing tool argument.                         | Re-read schema; do not retry blindly.            |
| `UnexpectedFailure`         | Bug in the MCP server itself.                      | Capture `details`, surface to the user, do not retry. |

## Cross-platform parity protocol

When a feature must work on both Android and iOS, run the **same** test case against each device and compare. Use the same selectors (`text=`, `resource_id=`) — they map to UIA2 (`text` / `resourceId`) on Android and WDA (`labelContains` / `name`) on iOS via the composite repos.

Parity checklist:

1. The test passes on both serials in the same `new_session`.
2. The screenshots show the same screen at the same step (manually compare; deferred: visual diff).
3. The log evidence (`read_logs`) shows the same business-event tags on both platforms.
4. Differences in `os_version` are noted in the report.
5. Tools that aren't supported on iOS (`clear_app_data`, `grant_permission`, `start_recording`) are replaced with a documented workaround in the test plan — **never silently skipped**.

If a test passes on one platform but is "not applicable" on the other, that's a spec answer (e.g. "iOS uses Face ID instead of fingerprint") — record the AC mapping for both platforms separately rather than claiming parity.

## Reporting

Every test session run through this skill ends with a report block:

```
SESSION:    <session_id>            ← from `new_session`
DEVICE:     <serial> (<platform> <os_version>)
ARTIFACTS:  <path>                  ← from `get_artifacts_dir`

PASSED:    <n>
FAILED:    <n>
ERRORED:   <n>
SKIPPED:   <n>  (reasons must be listed)

AC COVERAGE
  AC-1  ✓  TC-HOME-01
  AC-2  ✗  TC-AUTH-07     →  see screenshots/auth-fail.png + logs/auth-fail.log
  AC-3  ⚠  no test         →  GAP

REWRITES NEEDED
  TC-XYZ-04 — fails Rubric criterion 3 (uses Future.delayed)
  TC-XYZ-09 — fails Rubric criterion 1 (no AC mapped)

TRIAGE
  TC-AUTH-07 → TimeoutFailure on wait_for("Welcome", 10s)
              → screenshot shows network error toast
              → read_logs: "AuthRepo: 401 from /me"
              → Likely real bug, not test-side.
```

The report is the deliverable. Without it, "ran the tests on a phone" is not evidence anyone can act on.

## Quick decision tree

```
Is this an AC or a hunch?
  hunch  → don't write a hardware test; explore manually first
  AC     ↓

Can a unit/widget test prove it?
  yes    → push it down the pyramid; don't use the MCP
  no     ↓

Does it pass all 10 Rubric criteria?
  no     → REWRITE before running
  yes    ↓

Pick MCP loop A/B/C/D/E
Run on Android serial
Cross-platform feature?
  yes    → run identical case on iOS serial; compare evidence
  no     ↓

Produce the SESSION report. Map every result to its AC.
```

## What this skill will NOT do

- Decide for you whether a feature ships. The skill produces evidence; the human owns the call.
- Pretend a flaky test is "intermittent". Flake = Rubric criterion 3 violation = rewrite.
- Run destructive operations (factory reset, `uninstall` on production-data apps) without explicit user confirmation.
- Silently mock anything for a "hardware" run. Mocks belong below this layer; if a hardware test needs a mock, the test is misplaced.

content.text:
Both deliverables shipped.

## iOS support — what changed

- **Domain (additive):** `Platform` enum, `Device.platform` + `os_version` (renamed from `android_version`), `AppBundle` (renamed from `ApkArtifact`) carries `platform`. Two repository methods renamed for cross-platform clarity: `BuildRepository.build_apk → build_bundle`, `LifecycleRepository.install(apk_path) → install(bundle_path)`. No use case lost any capability.
- **Infrastructure:** `PyMobileDevice3Cli` (CLI wrapper for usbmux/install/launch/screenshot/syslog), `CachingWdaFactory` (facebook-wda over usbmux — no iproxy needed), `FlutterCli.build_ipa`.
- **Data:** `parse_usbmux_list`, `IosDeviceRepository`, `IosLifecycleRepository`, `WdaUiRepository`, `IosObservationRepository`. `FlutterBuildRepository` now branches by platform; `FlutterTestRepository` is platform-agnostic.
- **Composite layer (the routing core):** `CachingPlatformResolver` maps serial → `Platform`. `CompositeDeviceRepository` unions both backends and warms the cache. `CompositeLifecycle/Ui/Observation/TestRepository` resolve serial → impl per call. `CompositeBuildRepository` routes by explicit `platform` arg. iOS toolchain absence does not break Android paths.
- **Container:** wires Android + iOS concretes behind the composites. Use cases never see platform routing.
- **Presentation:** `build_app` and `install_app` schemas gained an optional `platform` field. All other 26 tools work identically on both platforms. iOS-unsupported operations (`clear_data`, `grant_permission`, screen recording) return typed `Failure`s with hints rather than silently no-op'ing.
- **Tests:** 18 new tests (6 for `parse_usbmux_list`, 12 for composite routing including a "iOS toolchain failure must not block Android" case). **44/44 pass.**

## Testing skill

Created at [`~/Desktop/claude_skills/skills/mcp-phone-controll-testing/SKILL.md`](~/Desktop/claude_skills/skills/mcp-phone-controll-testing/SKILL.md). It encodes:

- **Trigger conditions** in the frontmatter `description` so Claude loads it when the user is planning, executing, or reviewing on-device tests.
- **Test Validity Rubric** — 10 concrete criteria (mapped-to-AC, single-behaviour, deterministic, observable-via-MCP, isolated, idempotent setup, bounded waits, cross-platform-safe, failure-aware, cheap-diagnosis) with explicit **rewrite signals** that flag invalid cases before they're wasted on hardware.
- **Requirement → test traceability matrix** to surface coverage gaps and over-coarse cases.
- **Test pyramid placement** rules to push misplaced concerns down (or up).
- **Five canonical MCP loop patterns** (smoke, single-AC integration, negative path, async, suite run) — Claude picks the smallest that proves the AC.
- **Failure-triage cheat sheet** keyed to every `Failure.code` the MCP returns, with the next tool to call for each.
- **Cross-platform parity protocol** for features shipping on both platforms.
- **Required reporting block** — every session ends with a structured report mapping passed/failed cases to AC IDs, listing rewrites needed, and citing screenshot/log evidence for triage.

content.text:
Three layers of pre-flight checks, increasing realism — none need Claude Code or a phone.

## 1. Run the test suite (already green)

```bash
cd /Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll
source .venv/bin/activate
pytest -v
```

44 tests against fakes — proves wiring, routing, and use-case logic without subprocess.

## 2. Drive the dispatcher from a Python REPL

This skips the MCP protocol entirely and calls your tool dispatcher directly. Best for catching bugs in real repositories with a phone plugged in.

```bash
.venv/bin/python -c "
import asyncio, json
from mcp_phone_controll.container import build_descriptors
from mcp_phone_controll.presentation.tool_registry import ToolDispatcher

async def main():
    d = ToolDispatcher(build_descriptors())
    print(json.dumps(await d.dispatch('list_devices', {}), indent=2))

asyncio.run(main())
"
```

You should get `{ok: true, data: [...]}` with whatever ADB sees (or empty array if no device). Swap the tool name + args to try anything: `select_device`, `take_screenshot`, etc.

## 3. Speak MCP over stdio (closest to what Claude will do)

Easiest is the official inspector — it gives you a UI:

```bash
npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector .venv/bin/python -m mcp_phone_controll
```

Browser opens, you can list/call every tool, see the schema, and inspect the JSON envelope. This is exactly the protocol Claude Code uses, just with a human in the loop.

If you'd rather not pull in npx, raw JSON-RPC works too:

```bash
(echo '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"initialize","params":{"protocolVersion":"2024-11-05","capabilities":{},"clientInfo":{"name":"manual","version":"0"}}}'; \
 echo '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"notifications/initialized"}'; \
 echo '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"tools/list"}') \
| .venv/bin/python -m mcp_phone_controll
```

You should see the 28 tools in the response.

## What to verify at each layer

| Layer | Catches |
|-------|---------|
| **1. pytest** | logic bugs, routing, schema errors, parser bugs |
| **2. REPL** | real-device interaction (adb / pymobiledevice3 / WDA reachable, app builds, etc.) |
| **3. MCP inspector** | protocol-level issues — tool registration, schema validation, message framing |

When all three pass, registering with `claude mcp add` is just paperwork. **Recommendation:** run #1 now, #2 once you have a phone plugged in, #3 only if either of the first two surfaces something odd.

content: how can i test if reach my phones as android or ios, how to test it before gibing it to claude?

message.content: how can i test if reach my phones as android or ios, how to test it before gibing it to claude?

content.text:
Two quick commands — one for each platform — to confirm the MCP can actually see your devices before you wire it into Claude.

## Android — does ADB see the phone?

Plug in over USB, unlock the phone, accept the "Trust this computer" prompt, then:

```bash
adb devices -l
```

You should see something like `EMU01    device  product:... model:Pixel_7`. If it says `unauthorized` → unlock and re-tap Trust. If empty → cable, USB-debugging toggle, or driver issue. **If `adb devices` doesn't see it, the MCP won't either** — fix this first.

Then drive the MCP's Android path directly:

```bash
cd /Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll
.venv/bin/python -c "
import asyncio, json
from mcp_phone_controll.container import build_use_cases
from mcp_phone_controll.domain.usecases.base import NoParams
from mcp_phone_controll.presentation.serialization import to_jsonable

async def main():
    uc = build_use_cases()
    res = await uc.list_devices(NoParams())
    print(json.dumps(to_jsonable(res.value if res.is_ok else res.failure), indent=2))

asyncio.run(main())
"
```

Each device should come back with `\"platform\": \"android\"` and an `os_version`.

## iOS — does pymobiledevice3 see the iPhone?

Plug in via USB, unlock, tap "Trust" on the phone, then:

```bash
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 usbmux list
```

You should get a JSON array with your device's `Identifier`, `ProductType`, `ProductVersion`. Empty or error → either the iPhone isn't trusted, or `usbmuxd` isn't running (it's part of macOS, normally always on — a reboot fixes the rare case where it isn't).

Then run the same `list_devices` call as above. If both phones are plugged in, you'll get a unioned list:

```json
[
  {"serial": "EMU01", "platform": "android", "os_version": "14", ...},
  {"serial": "00008101-001A...", "platform": "ios", "os_version": "17.4.1", ...}
]
```

## A one-shot sanity script

Drop this into `scripts/preflight.py` and run it any time you want a green light:

```bash
mkdir -p scripts && cat > scripts/preflight.py <<'PY'
import asyncio, json
from mcp_phone_controll.container import build_use_cases
from mcp_phone_controll.domain.usecases.base import NoParams
from mcp_phone_controll.presentation.serialization import to_jsonable

async def main():
    uc = build_use_cases()
    res = await uc.list_devices(NoParams())
    if res.is_err:
        print("FAIL:", res.failure.message); return
    if not res.value:
        print("No devices. Check USB, unlock screen, accept Trust prompt."); return
    for d in res.value:
        print(f"  {d.platform.value:7s}  {d.serial:40s}  {d.model or '-':25s}  {d.os_version or '-'}")

asyncio.run(main())
PY
.venv/bin/python scripts/preflight.py
```

Run it with each phone individually first, then both plugged in. When you see one row per device with the correct platform tag, the MCP is ready — `claude mcp add` is the only step left.

## If a device is missing

| Symptom | Most likely cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Android phone absent | USB debugging off, or host not authorised | Settings → Developer options → USB debugging; replug; tap Trust |
| Android shows `unauthorized` | First-time host fingerprint not accepted | Unlock phone, replug, tap Allow |
| iPhone absent | Not trusted, or locked | Unlock, replug, tap Trust on phone |
| iPhone shows but launch later fails | Developer Disk Image not mounted | `.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 mounter auto-mount` |
| Both empty, both phones plugged | Wrong cable (charge-only) | Use a data USB cable |

Once `preflight.py` lists every device you expect, you've verified the same code path Claude will hit — handover is safe.

content.text:
That's the architecture working correctly — a missing system tool surfaced as a typed `UnexpectedFailure` envelope instead of crashing. Two fixes needed before you'll see your phone:

## Install the toolchains

```bash
brew install android-platform-tools          # gives you `adb`
```

`pymobiledevice3` is already in your venv (it shipped with the project), so iOS is ready. Android needs the platform tools from Homebrew because they're a separate Google distribution, not a Python package.


content.text:
The `brew install` either hasn't run yet or the new shell PATH hasn't loaded. Quick check + fix:

```bash
brew install --cask android-platform-tools
```

Note the `--cask` — on Homebrew, `android-platform-tools` ships as a cask, not a formula. (Without `--cask`, brew silently does nothing if the formula doesn't exist.)

Then in the **same terminal**:

```bash
hash -r           # forget cached "not found" lookups
which adb && adb version
```

You should see something like `/opt/homebrew/bin/adb` (Apple Silicon) or `/usr/local/bin/adb` (Intel) and a version string.

If `which adb` is *still* empty:

```bash
ls /opt/homebrew/Caskroom/android-platform-tools/*/platform-tools/adb 2>/dev/null \
  || ls /usr/local/Caskroom/android-platform-tools/*/platform-tools/adb 2>/dev/null
```

Whichever path that prints, symlink it or add to PATH:

```bash
echo 'export PATH="/opt/homebrew/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.zshrc && source ~/.zshrc
```

Once `adb version` works, re-run the `list_devices` one-liner — empty array if no phone, populated if one is plugged in.

Yes — that's a clean, working Android pipeline end-to-end. Your Galaxy S25 (`SM_S931B`, Android 16) was discovered via ADB, enriched with `os_version` via `getprop`, tagged `platform: android` by the resolver, and serialised through the dispatcher into the exact envelope Claude will receive. Everything from infrastructure → data → composite → presentation is alive.

## Quick smoke before you hand it to Claude

Run these in order against the same device. Each should return `"ok": true`:

```bash
SERIAL=R3CYA05CHXB

.venv/bin/python -c "
import asyncio, json, sys
from mcp_phone_controll.container import build_descriptors
from mcp_phone_controll.presentation.tool_registry import ToolDispatcher

async def call(d, name, args=None):
    r = await d.dispatch(name, args or {})
    print(name, '→', json.dumps(r)[:200])
    return r

async def main():
    d = ToolDispatcher(build_descriptors())
    await call(d, 'select_device', {'serial': '$SERIAL'})
    await call(d, 'get_selected_device')
    await call(d, 'new_session', {'label': 'preflight'})
    await call(d, 'take_screenshot', {'label': 'home'})
    await call(d, 'read_logs', {'since_s': 5, 'min_level': 'W', 'max_lines': 20})

asyncio.run(main())
"
```

What each line proves:

- **`select_device`** — session state holds the serial.
- **`get_selected_device`** — round-trip is intact.
- **`new_session`** — filesystem artifact dir was created under `~/.mcp_phone_controll/sessions/`.
- **`take_screenshot`** — `adb exec-out screencap` works AND the artifact was written to the session dir. The returned `data` is the path; `ls` it to confirm.
- **`read_logs`** — logcat parsing works against real device output.

If any of those error out, paste the envelope and we'll fix it before Claude touches it. If all five are `"ok": true`, you have a complete green light to register:

```bash
claude mcp add phone-controll -- /Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll/.venv/bin/python -m mcp_phone_controll
```

(iOS UI automation will need WebDriverAgent built on the device once you plug an iPhone in, but that's a separate step — Android alone is fully ready right now.)

content.text:
Android is fully green. For iOS:

## 1. Plug in the iPhone

USB cable, unlock the phone, tap **Trust This Computer** when prompted. (Pymobiledevice3 won't see the device until you trust it.)

## 2. Confirm pymobiledevice3 sees it

```bash
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 usbmux list
```

You should get a JSON array with one entry — copy the `Identifier` value (a 25- or 40-char string like `00008101-001A4D2A14C8001E`).

## 3. Run `list_devices` through the MCP — should now show both phones

```bash
.venv/bin/python -c "
import asyncio, json
from mcp_phone_controll.container import build_descriptors
from mcp_phone_controll.presentation.tool_registry import ToolDispatcher

async def main():
    d = ToolDispatcher(build_descriptors())
    print(json.dumps(await d.dispatch('list_devices', {}), indent=2))

asyncio.run(main())
"
```

You'll get one Android row and one iOS row. The iOS one will have `"platform": "ios"` and `"os_version": "17.x.x"`.

## 4. iOS smoke — same shape as the Android one

Set `IOS_UDID` to the identifier from step 2, then:

```bash
IOS_UDID=00008101-XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX

.venv/bin/python -c "
import asyncio, json
from mcp_phone_controll.container import build_descriptors
from mcp_phone_controll.presentation.tool_registry import ToolDispatcher

async def call(d, name, args=None):
    r = await d.dispatch(name, args or {})
    print(name, '→', json.dumps(r)[:200])

async def main():
    d = ToolDispatcher(build_descriptors())
    await call(d, 'list_devices')                                  # warms platform cache
    await call(d, 'select_device', {'serial': '$IOS_UDID'})
    await call(d, 'get_selected_device')
    await call(d, 'new_session', {'label': 'ios-preflight'})
    await call(d, 'take_screenshot', {'label': 'ios-home'})
    await call(d, 'read_logs', {'since_s': 3, 'min_level': 'W', 'max_lines': 20})

asyncio.run(main())
"
```

What proves what:

- **`list_devices` first** — required before anything else, because the composite resolver learns "this UDID = iOS" from this call. (Your Android run skipped this only because `select_device` falls back to probing both backends; iOS users typically get faster routing if they list first.)
- **`take_screenshot`** — exercises `pymobiledevice3 screenshot`. Doesn't need a Developer Disk Image; works with just the trust handshake. The PNG lands in your session dir.
- **`read_logs`** — exercises `pymobiledevice3 syslog collect`. Will block for ~3 seconds (the `since_s` window) on iOS — that's expected behaviour, not a hang.

## What to expect to *fail* (and why it's fine)

These need extra steps you haven't done yet, and are safe to defer:

- `launch_app` / `stop_app` / `run_integration_tests` — need the **Developer Disk Image** mounted. One-time setup: `.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 mounter auto-mount`. After that, launch works.
- `tap`, `tap_text`, `swipe`, etc. — need **WebDriverAgent built and installed** on the device via Xcode. Search "WebDriverAgent install" — it's a one-time Xcode build per device.
- `start_recording` — returns a typed "not supported on iOS" failure by design.
- `clear_app_data` / `grant_permission` — same; iOS doesn't expose these primitives.

So for the preflight, **`list_devices` + `select_device` + `take_screenshot` + `read_logs` is the right scope**. If those four are `"ok": true`, your iOS pipeline is live and ready to hand to Claude. The DDI mount and WDA build are optional unlocks for richer flows later.

iOS device isn't being discovered — the composite quietly skipped it because the iOS backend returned an empty/unparseable list. Two debug steps to find which:

## 1. What does `pymobiledevice3` actually print?

```bash

```

If this is empty or errors → the iPhone isn't being seen by pymobiledevice3 itself. Make sure it's plugged in, unlocked, "Trust" tapped, then re-run.

If it shows your device but in a **table format** (not JSON), our parser is rejecting it. Run this to see the raw shape:

```bash
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 usbmux list --no-color
```

Paste the output here — I likely need to add `--json` to the CLI invocation. The `pymobiledevice3` CLI changed its default output format across versions and I bet on the wrong one.

## 2. Confirm the MCP-side iOS path in isolation

```bash
.venv/bin/python -c "
import asyncio, json
from mcp_phone_controll.infrastructure.process_runner import AsyncProcessRunner
from mcp_phone_controll.infrastructure.pymobiledevice3_cli import PyMobileDevice3Cli
from mcp_phone_controll.data.repositories.ios_device_repository import IosDeviceRepository

async def main():
    cli = PyMobileDevice3Cli(AsyncProcessRunner())
    raw = await cli.usbmux_list()
    print('returncode:', raw.returncode)
    print('stdout (first 500 chars):', repr(raw.stdout[:500]))
    print('stderr (first 200 chars):', repr(raw.stderr[:200]))
    print()
    repo = IosDeviceRepository(cli)
    res = await repo.list_devices()
    print('parsed result:', res)

asyncio.run(main())
"
```

Paste both outputs and I'll patch the CLI flag / parser accordingly. Most likely fix is one line: `["usbmux", "list", "--no-color"]` → `["usbmux", "list", "--no-color", "--usb"]` or `+ ["--json"]` depending on what your installed pymobiledevice3 version emits.

All 44 tests still pass. Now re-run your iOS preflight — it should work now:

```bash
IOS_UDID=00008120-001A42542E30201E

.venv/bin/python -c "
import asyncio, json

from mcp_phone_controll.presentation.tool_registry import ToolDispatcher

async def call(d, name, args=None):

async def main():
    d = ToolDispatcher(build_descriptors())
    await call(d, 'list_devices')
    await call(d, 'select_device', {'serial': '$IOS_UDID'})
    await call(d, 'get_selected_device')
    await call(d, 'new_session', {'label': 'ios-preflight'})
    await call(d, 'take_screenshot', {'label': 'ios-home'})
    await call(d, 'read_logs', {'since_s': 3, 'min_level': 'W', 'max_lines': 20})

asyncio.run(main())
"
```

`list_devices` should now return both your Galaxy S25 and the iPhone 14 Pro. The screenshot will be PNG-encoded by `pymobiledevice3 developer screenshot` into your session dir. `read_logs` will block ~3 seconds collecting syslog.

If `take_screenshot` fails with something like "DDI not mounted", that's expected — `pymobiledevice3 developer screenshot` does need the Developer Disk Image. One-time fix:

```bash
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 mounter auto-mount
```

Then re-run. Paste anything that's not `"ok": true` and I'll patch it.

content.text:
Great progress — discovery and selection are clean. Two real iOS issues exposed by your iOS 26 device:

## 1. Screenshot — needs a developer tunnel (iOS 17+)

The `Trying again over tunnel` warning is pymobiledevice3 telling you the screenshot service moved to RemoteXPC on iOS 17+, which requires an **active tunnel running as root**. This is an iOS-side architectural change Apple made — not a bug in the MCP. Start the tunnel once in a separate terminal and leave it running:

```bash
sudo .venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 remote start-tunnel
```

Keep that terminal open. From any other terminal, `take_screenshot`, `launch_app`, etc. will work.

(`pymobiledevice3 mounter auto-mount` may also be needed once for DDI-backed services like launch — it's a one-time-per-iOS-version operation.)

## 2. Syslog — our flag set is wrong for current pymobiledevice3

Find the actual flags:

```bash
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 syslog collect --help
```

Paste the output and I'll patch the wrapper. Most likely the `--timeout` flag was renamed or removed. Quickest options I'd consider once we see help:

- **If `-t` exists:** change `--timeout` → `-t`.
- **If no timeout flag at all:** switch to the streaming approach — start `pymobiledevice3 syslog live`, kill the subprocess after `since_s`, parse what we got. Cleaner anyway because it doesn't need a tunnel.

## Sanity check before that — what's the full stderr?

Your console truncated. Run this to see the complete error from both calls:

```bash
.venv/bin/python -c "
import asyncio, json
from mcp_phone_controll.container import build_descriptors
from mcp_phone_controll.presentation.tool_registry import ToolDispatcher

async def main():
    d = ToolDispatcher(build_descriptors())
    await d.dispatch('list_devices', {})
    await d.dispatch('select_device', {'serial': '00008120-001A42542E30201E'})
    await d.dispatch('new_session', {'label': 'diag'})
    s = await d.dispatch('take_screenshot', {})
    l = await d.dispatch('read_logs', {'since_s': 2})
    print('SCREENSHOT'); print(json.dumps(s, indent=2))
    print('LOGS'); print(json.dumps(l, indent=2))

asyncio.run(main())
"
```

Paste both blocks plus the `syslog collect --help` output and I'll roll fixes for both. Net: discovery/selection/sessions are solid on both platforms; the remaining work is wiring two modern-iOS quirks into the iOS observation repo.

content.text:
Two clear fixes — one external (start the right tunnel daemon), one in the code (replace the broken syslog wrapper with streaming).

## Fix 1: screenshot — start `tunneld` (daemon, not `start-tunnel`)

In a separate terminal, leave this running:

```bash
sudo .venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 remote tunneld
```

That's the command pymobiledevice3 itself recommended in the error. `start-tunnel` is a different (and on iOS 26 currently broken) flow. Once `tunneld` is up, screenshot will work.

## Fix 2: syslog — `collect` is the wrong subcommand entirely

`syslog collect` produces a `.logarchive` directory for macOS `log show` — not what we want. The right primitive is `syslog live` (streaming), which we run as a subprocess and tear down after the requested window. Patching now:

content.text:
Big win: **`read_logs` now works** — `ok: true` with an empty array means the live syslog ran for 3s and nothing at WARN level showed up (iOS was quiet — try `min_level: "I"` and `since_s: 5` to confirm you'll see traffic).

Screenshot needs three iOS-side prerequisites, **all of which must be true at once**. The error message confirms it ("Make sure you first enabled..." — that's the start of the Developer Mode hint):

## iOS prerequisite checklist

### 1. Developer Mode ON (one-time, per device)

On the iPhone: **Settings → Privacy & Security → Developer Mode → On**. The phone reboots and asks for confirmation after restart. **Required since iOS 16** for any developer-tier service (screenshot, launch, syslog over tunnel, etc.).

If you don't see the menu item: connect the phone to Xcode once or run any pymobiledevice3 developer command — that makes the toggle appear.

### 2. Developer Disk Image mounted (one-time per iOS major version)

```bash
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 mounter auto-mount
```

Should print "Mounted developer image" or "Already mounted".

### 3. `tunneld` daemon running (every session, in another terminal)

```bash
sudo /Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll/.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 remote tunneld
```

Leave that terminal open. The earlier `start-tunnel` you tried hit `QUIC protocol error` — that's fine, it's a different command. **`tunneld` is the daemon screenshot wants.**

## Verify with the bare CLI before re-running the MCP

This bypasses our code so you know exactly which step is missing:

```bash
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 developer screenshot --udid $IOS_UDID /tmp/ios.png
```

- ✅ Works → file at `/tmp/ios.png`. Then re-run the MCP smoke; it'll pass.
- ❌ "Developer mode is not enabled" → step 1.
- ❌ "DeveloperDiskImage not mounted" → step 2.
- ❌ "Unable to connect to Tunneld" → step 3.

Once that one CLI call succeeds, every iOS service (screenshot, launch, kill, syslog over tunnel) works. **The MCP itself is now correct on the iOS side** — what's left is purely device configuration.

## What's already green for handover to Claude

- ✅ Both platforms discovered and resolver-cached
- ✅ Device selection across platforms
- ✅ Sessions / artifact dirs
- ✅ Android: screenshot, logs, build, install, launch, UI driving
- ✅ iOS: device listing, selection, **read_logs (just fixed)**
- ⚠️ iOS screenshot/launch/UI: works as soon as Developer Mode + DDI + tunneld are set up

If you want, you can register with Claude **right now** for Android use and worry about iOS Developer Mode later — the composite layer means Claude can use Android tools without iOS being fully provisioned.

## Order of operations

```bash
# 1. Enable Developer Mode on the phone (interactive — will prompt you, then reboot the device)
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 amfi enable-developer-mode --udid $IOS_UDID

# 2. After the iPhone reboots and you flip the toggle in Settings → Privacy & Security → Developer Mode → On:
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 mounter auto-mount --udid $IOS_UDID

# 3. In a SEPARATE terminal — leave running:
sudo /Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll/.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 remote tunneld

# 4. Back in this terminal — sanity check via the bare CLI:
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 developer screenshot --tunnel $IOS_UDID /tmp/ios.png && open /tmp/ios.png

# 5. Then re-run the MCP smoke (same script as before):
IOS_UDID=00008120-001A42542E30201E .venv/bin/python -c "
import asyncio, json
from mcp_phone_controll.container import build_descriptors
from mcp_phone_controll.presentation.tool_registry import ToolDispatcher
```

If step 4 still complains about Developer Mode, the toggle in **Settings → Privacy & Security → Developer Mode** isn't on yet — that one is manual after the reboot. Apple's design, not ours.

```bash
cd /Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll && source .venv/bin/activate
IOS_UDID=00008120-001A42542E30201E
```

### Step 1 — enable Developer Mode

```bash
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 amfi enable-developer-mode --udid $IOS_UDID
```

The phone will reboot. After it boots back up: **Settings → Privacy & Security → Developer Mode → On → enter passcode → confirm**. Don't proceed until that toggle is on.

### Step 2 — mount the Developer Disk Image

```bash
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 mounter auto-mount --udid $IOS_UDID
```

Should print `Mounted` (or `Already mounted`).

### Step 3 — bare CLI screenshot test (proves all three prereqs are satisfied)

```bash
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 developer screenshot --tunnel $IOS_UDID /tmp/ios.png && open /tmp/ios.png
```

Preview should open showing your iPhone home screen. If it does → every iOS service the MCP exposes will work. If it errors, paste the error and we'll narrow down which prereq slipped.

```bash
asyncio.run(main())
"
```

```bash

```

And from then on Claude can drive both phones through one toolset. Just remember: the `tunneld` terminal needs to keep running for any iOS developer-tier call (screenshot, launch, kill). Set a reminder, or `launchctl`-ify it later.

content.text:
Same error → one of the prereqs still isn't satisfied. Don't guess — query each one and pinpoint which is missing:

```bash
# Tunneld — confirm it's reachable and has your device
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:49151/ | head -50

# Developer Mode status on the phone
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 amfi developer-mode-status --udid $IOS_UDID

# DDI mount status
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 mounter list --udid $IOS_UDID
```

Paste the output of all three and we'll know exactly what's missing. The most common cause at this point is the **manual Settings toggle**: `amfi enable-developer-mode` only *triggers the option to appear* — you still have to physically:

1. Reboot the phone (it should have done this already)
2. Open **Settings → Privacy & Security**
3. Scroll to **Developer Mode**
4. Toggle it **On**
5. The phone will reboot a second time and ask for confirmation
6. Enter your passcode and tap **Turn On**

After all that, `amfi developer-mode-status` should print `Developer Mode is enabled: True`. If it prints `False`, the toggle hasn't actually been flipped despite the `enable-developer-mode` call.

Run the three diagnostics and paste — we'll have the answer in one round.

content.text:
`read_logs` now returns real Bluetooth telemetry from the iPhone — that proves tunneld is healthy and reachable. So the missing prereq for screenshot is narrower: it's almost certainly **DDI not mounted** (screenshot is a developer-tier service that needs the disk image; syslog isn't).

Run the three diagnostics I asked about earlier — the DDI one will give us the answer:

```bash
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 amfi developer-mode-status --udid $IOS_UDID
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 mounter list --udid $IOS_UDID
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 mounter auto-mount --udid $IOS_UDID
```

Paste all three outputs. Most likely outcome:

- `developer-mode-status` → `True` (since logs already work over tunnel).
- `mounter list` → empty or no Personalized image listed → that's our culprit.
- `mounter auto-mount` → either succeeds and prints "Mounted", or errors with a specific reason (network, Apple TSS unreachable, etc.).

After `auto-mount` succeeds, re-run:

```bash
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 developer screenshot --tunnel $IOS_UDID /tmp/ios.png && open /tmp/ios.png
```

That bare CLI call has to work first — only then does the MCP screenshot. If `auto-mount` itself errors, paste that error and we'll diagnose it specifically (sometimes Apple's image server is rate-limiting; sometimes the device needs to be unlocked at the moment of mount).

lastPrompt: (mcp_phone_controll) michalgiza@Michals-MacBook-Pro mcp_phone_controll % .venv/bin/python -c " import asyncio, json from mcp_phone_controll.container import build_descriptors from mcp_phone_controll.p…

content.text:
That's important diagnostic data — three things ARE working:

- ✅ Developer Mode: `true`
- ✅ DDI mounted: version `518.27` (Xcode-shipped iOS 17 DDI)
- ✅ Tunneld: routing fine (your `read_logs` over tunnel proved it)

**But your phone is iOS 26.2.1 and the mounted DDI is from iOS 17.** That's the gap — Xcode shipped you the iOS 17 DDI, auto-mount happily took it, but the iOS 26 OS likely needs an iOS 26-vintage DDI for the `screenshotr` service. The "Apple removed this service, or your iOS version does not support it" line in the error is real — when DDI and iOS major versions diverge, individual developer services start returning that.

Two avenues to fix it, in order of likelihood:

## 1. Update pymobiledevice3 first (cheapest)

```bash
.venv/bin/pip install -U pymobiledevice3
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 version
```

Then retry the bare CLI. Newer pymobiledevice3 releases often add iOS-version-specific service paths.

## 2. If that doesn't fix it, the DDI itself is stale

Check what Xcode you have:

```bash
xcodebuild -version
ls /Applications/Xcode*.app/Contents/Developer/Platforms/iPhoneOS.platform/DeviceSupport/ 2>/dev/null
```

If the highest folder there is `17.x (...)` your Xcode predates iOS 26 and **you need a newer Xcode for screenshot/launch on this phone.** App Store → Xcode → Update. That gets a fresh iOS 26 DDI on disk; pymobiledevice3's auto-mount will then pick it up.

You may also need to unmount the stale image first so auto-mount picks the new one:

```bash
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 mounter umount-developer --udid $IOS_UDID 2>/dev/null \
  || .venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 mounter umount --udid $IOS_UDID
.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3 mounter auto-mount --udid $IOS_UDID
```

## Pragmatic call

iOS 26 is a few weeks old; tooling lag on bleeding-edge iOS is real and outside the MCP. The MCP architecture is correct — the same `take_screenshot` call will work the moment the DDI/iOS gap closes.

**What's already production-ready right now:**

- Android end-to-end (your Galaxy S25): `take_screenshot`, `read_logs`, `tap_text`, `install_app`, etc. — fully working.
- iOS device discovery, selection, sessions, and `read_logs`.
- iOS install/uninstall (lockdown-tier, doesn't need DDI/tunnel) — should work without further setup.

**Suggestion:** register the MCP with Claude **now** for Android-first work, while you sort out the Xcode/DDI iOS upgrade in the background. The same registration line works for both platforms — when you fix iOS, those tools become available automatically without re-registering.

```bash
claude mcp add phone-controll -- /Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll/.venv/bin/python -m mcp_phone_controll
```

Try the pymobiledevice3 upgrade first — paste the output and we'll know if it's that or an Xcode update.

message.content: i think we should now pass ios as in initial plan i will focus on that after, how to test all on android as here and enable it to claude?

content.text:
Sensible call. Android is solid; iOS is a tooling-version problem to revisit later. Here's a complete Android verification plan, then the Claude registration.

## 1. One-time UIA2 setup on the phone

UI automation (`tap`, `tap_text`, `find_element`, etc.) needs the uiautomator2 agent APK installed on the device. Run once per device:

```bash
.venv/bin/python -m uiautomator2 init
```

Should report something like *"Successfully installed atx-agent"* on `R3CYA05CHXB`. After this, all UI tools work.

## 2. Full Android smoke — exercises every tool that matters

Paste-ready. Drives the Settings app (which is always installed) so it has no dependency on a Flutter project being available:

```bash
cd /Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll && source .venv/bin/activate
SERIAL=R3CYA05CHXB

.venv/bin/python <<'PY'
import asyncio, json, os
from mcp_phone_controll.container import build_descriptors
from mcp_phone_controll.presentation.tool_registry import ToolDispatcher

SERIAL = os.environ["SERIAL"]
PKG = "com.android.settings"

async def call(d, name, args=None):
    r = await d.dispatch(name, args or {})
    ok = "OK " if r["ok"] else "FAIL"
    head = json.dumps(r.get("data") if r["ok"] else r["error"])[:160]
    print(f"  {ok}  {name:22s}  {head}")
    return r

async def main():
    d = ToolDispatcher(build_descriptors())
    print("\n--- discovery & selection ---")
    await call(d, "list_devices")
    await call(d, "select_device", {"serial": SERIAL})
    await call(d, "get_selected_device")
    await call(d, "new_session", {"label": "android-full-smoke"})

    print("\n--- observation ---")
    await call(d, "take_screenshot", {"label": "before"})
    await call(d, "read_logs", {"since_s": 2, "min_level": "I", "max_lines": 5})

    print("\n--- lifecycle ---")
    await call(d, "stop_app",   {"package_id": PKG})
    await call(d, "launch_app", {"package_id": PKG})
    await asyncio.sleep(1.5)

    print("\n--- ui interaction ---")
    await call(d, "dump_ui")
    await call(d, "find_element", {"text": "Settings", "timeout_s": 5})
    await call(d, "wait_for_element", {"text": "Settings", "timeout_s": 5})
    await call(d, "assert_visible", {"text": "Settings", "timeout_s": 5})
    await call(d, "swipe", {"x1": 500, "y1": 1500, "x2": 500, "y2": 800, "duration_ms": 300})
    await call(d, "press_key", {"keycode": "back"})
    await call(d, "take_screenshot", {"label": "after"})

    print("\n--- recording (5s) ---")
    await call(d, "start_recording", {"label": "settings-tour"})
    await asyncio.sleep(3)
    await call(d, "stop_recording")

    print("\n--- cleanup ---")
    await call(d, "stop_app", {"package_id": PKG})
    await call(d, "get_artifacts_dir")

asyncio.run(main())
PY
```

Every line should print `OK`. Failures will print `FAIL` with a trimmed reason. Expected outcome on a healthy setup:

- 4× discovery/selection — all OK
- 2× observation — all OK
- 3× lifecycle — all OK
- 7× UI — all OK (`find_element` returns the element dict, others return null)
- 2× recording — all OK; an `.mp4` lands in the session dir
- 2× cleanup — all OK

Open the artifacts dir to eyeball the captures:

```bash
open "$(.venv/bin/python -c "from mcp_phone_controll.container import build_use_cases; import asyncio; uc=build_use_cases(); from mcp_phone_controll.domain.usecases.base import NoParams; r=asyncio.run(uc.get_artifacts_dir(NoParams())); print(r.value)")"
```

You should see `before.png`, `after.png`, and `settings-tour.mp4`.

## 3. Register with Claude

```bash
claude mcp add phone-controll -- /Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll/.venv/bin/python -m mcp_phone_controll
```

Verify it's registered:

```bash
claude mcp list
```

Should show `phone-controll` with status `connected` (or it'll connect on first tool call).

## 4. First-conversation prompt to give Claude

Open a fresh Claude Code session in any directory, paste this so Claude proves the wiring end-to-end:

```
You have an MCP called `phone-controll` for driving real Android phones.
Run a smoke check: list devices, select the Android one, start a new
session labeled "claude-handover", take a screenshot, launch
com.android.settings, wait for the text "Settings" to appear, take a
second screenshot, stop the app, and tell me where the artifacts are.
Don't proceed past any failure — show me the failure envelope verbatim.
```

If Claude completes that with two screenshots + an artifacts path, the integration is live and you can give it real work.

## What's deferred for iOS

When you come back to iOS:

1. Update Xcode (gets a fresh iOS 26 DDI).
2. `pip install -U pymobiledevice3` in the venv.
3. `mounter umount-developer` then `mounter auto-mount` to swap to the new DDI.
4. Re-run the iOS smoke from earlier — same script, same UDID. Should pass.

No code changes needed on the MCP side. The composite layer keeps Android working perfectly while iOS sits on the bench.

content.text:
Bash gotcha — `SERIAL=R3CYA05CHXB` is a shell-local variable, not exported, so the python subprocess doesn't see it. Two ways to fix:

```bash
export SERIAL=R3CYA05CHXB
```

then re-paste the whole heredoc. Or do it inline in one go:

```bash
SERIAL=R3CYA05CHXB .venv/bin/python <<'PY'
import asyncio, json, os
from mcp_phone_controll.container import build_descriptors
from mcp_phone_controll.presentation.tool_registry import ToolDispatcher

SERIAL = os.environ["SERIAL"]
PKG = "com.android.settings"

async def call(d, name, args=None):
    r = await d.dispatch(name, args or {})
    ok = "OK " if r["ok"] else "FAIL"
    head = json.dumps(r.get("data") if r["ok"] else r["error"])[:160]
    print(f"  {ok}  {name:22s}  {head}")
    return r

async def main():
    d = ToolDispatcher(build_descriptors())
    print("\n--- discovery & selection ---")
    await call(d, "list_devices")
    await call(d, "select_device", {"serial": SERIAL})
    await call(d, "get_selected_device")
    await call(d, "new_session", {"label": "android-full-smoke"})

    print("\n--- observation ---")
    await call(d, "take_screenshot", {"label": "before"})
    await call(d, "read_logs", {"since_s": 2, "min_level": "I", "max_lines": 5})

    print("\n--- lifecycle ---")
    await call(d, "stop_app",   {"package_id": PKG})
    await call(d, "launch_app", {"package_id": PKG})
    await asyncio.sleep(1.5)

    print("\n--- ui interaction ---")
    await call(d, "dump_ui")
    await call(d, "find_element",     {"text": "Settings", "timeout_s": 5})
    await call(d, "wait_for_element", {"text": "Settings", "timeout_s": 5})
    await call(d, "assert_visible",   {"text": "Settings", "timeout_s": 5})
    await call(d, "swipe", {"x1": 500, "y1": 1500, "x2": 500, "y2": 800, "duration_ms": 300})
    await call(d, "press_key", {"keycode": "back"})
    await call(d, "take_screenshot", {"label": "after"})

    print("\n--- recording (3s) ---")
    await call(d, "start_recording", {"label": "settings-tour"})
    await asyncio.sleep(3)
    await call(d, "stop_recording")

    print("\n--- cleanup ---")
    await call(d, "stop_app", {"package_id": PKG})
    await call(d, "get_artifacts_dir")

asyncio.run(main())
PY
```

Note the `SERIAL=R3CYA05CHXB` prefix on the same line as `python` — that exports it just for that command.

Paste that and report what passes/fails. If `find_element`/`wait_for_element` fail with a UIA2 connection error, run `python -m uiautomator2 init` first and retry.

content.text:
17/19 green — and the 2 failures aren't MCP bugs, they're **environmental**. Look at the `dump_ui` output: it starts with `com.android.systemui:id/n…`. That's the System UI / lock screen / notification shade — not the Settings activity. So Settings launched in the background but the lock screen was in front. The MCP correctly reported "Settings text not visible" because it genuinely wasn't.

Two likely causes (either or both):

1. **Phone was locked or the screen had timed out.** Wake + unlock it before running.
2. **System UI not in English.** Your iPhone is set to "Michał" (Polish locale). If your Galaxy is also Polish, the heading text is **"Ustawienia"**, not "Settings".

Quick re-run with both fixes — unlock the phone first, then:

```bash
SERIAL=R3CYA05CHXB .venv/bin/python <<'PY'
import asyncio, json, os
from mcp_phone_controll.container import build_descriptors
from mcp_phone_controll.presentation.tool_registry import ToolDispatcher

async def main():
    d = ToolDispatcher(build_descriptors())
    await d.dispatch("select_device", {"serial": os.environ["SERIAL"]})
    await d.dispatch("press_key", {"keycode": "home"})       # ensure home screen
    await d.dispatch("launch_app", {"package_id": "com.android.settings"})
    await asyncio.sleep(1.5)
    for label in ("Settings", "Ustawienia"):                 # try both languages
        r = await d.dispatch("find_element", {"text": label, "timeout_s": 3})
        print(label, "→", json.dumps(r)[:200])

asyncio.run(main())
PY
```

Whichever label returns a non-null element is the one your phone uses. Either way, **the MCP itself is working correctly** — finding nothing when nothing is there is the right behaviour.

## You're done — register with Claude

```bash
claude mcp add phone-controll -- /Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll/.venv/bin/python -m mcp_phone_controll
claude mcp list
```

Then in any Claude Code session, paste this to verify Claude can drive your phone end-to-end:

```
You have an MCP called `phone-controll` for driving real Android phones.
Run a smoke check:
  1. list_devices and tell me what you see
  2. select the Android device
  3. start a new session labeled "claude-handover"
  4. press_key home, then take_screenshot labeled "home"
  5. launch com.android.settings, wait 2 seconds, take_screenshot labeled "settings"
  6. dump_ui and tell me 3 visible texts you can see
  7. stop_app for com.android.settings
  8. get_artifacts_dir and report it
Stop on any failure and show me the failure envelope verbatim.
```

If Claude returns two screenshots and three real on-screen labels (in whatever language your phone is in), the integration is green and ready for real work. Welcome to a Claude that can actually use your phone.

content.text:
Two findings — and one of them is a real bug in our code:

1. **Phone is Polish** ✅ — "Ustawienia" was found, confirming Settings did launch and the phone is in Polish.
2. **A `selector.info` property bug in `UiAutomator2UiRepository`** ❌ — modern `uiautomator2` (v3+) made `.info` a property, but our code passed it to `asyncio.to_thread(selector.info)` as if it were a method. The property evaluates to a dict on the main thread, then `to_thread` tries to *call* the dict → "'dict' object is not callable". The first "Settings" lookup didn't hit it (it returned False at `wait`), but "Ustawienia" succeeded and triggered the broken line. Patching now:

content.text:
Ah — that screenshot is **Claude Desktop**, not Claude Code (the CLI). Different app, different config location. The `claude mcp add` command I gave you is for the CLI; for the desktop app you edit a JSON file directly.

## Exact steps

1. **Click "Edit Config"** in that screen — it opens `~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json` in your default editor.

2. **Paste this content** (overwriting whatever is there if it's empty `{}`):

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "phone-controll": {
      "command": "/Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll/.venv/bin/python",
      "args": ["-m", "mcp_phone_controll"],
      "env": {
        "PATH": "/opt/homebrew/bin:/opt/homebrew/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

The `env.PATH` is critical — when Claude Desktop spawns the server it doesn't inherit your shell's PATH, so `adb` (from Homebrew) wouldn't be findable without it. The path above covers Apple Silicon Homebrew + standard system locations.

3. **Save the file.**

4. **Quit Claude Desktop completely** (⌘Q — not just close the window) and reopen it. The settings page is read at startup; it doesn't hot-reload.

5. Re-open Settings → Local MCP servers. You should see `phone-controll` listed, ideally with a green dot.

## Verify

In a new conversation, ask:

```
Use the phone-controll MCP. Call list_devices and show me the result.
```

Claude should return your Galaxy S25 (and the iPhone). If it can't find the tools, click into the `phone-controll` row in settings — Claude Desktop shows the spawn error there (typically a Python import or PATH issue).

## Common first-time gotchas

- **"command not found"** in the server status: the `command` path is wrong. Verify with `ls /Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll/.venv/bin/python` — that exact path must exist.
- **"No module named mcp_phone_controll"**: the package isn't installed in that venv. Re-run `uv pip install -e ".[dev]"` from the project dir.
- **`adb not found` in tool error envelopes**: the `env.PATH` is missing or wrong. On Intel Macs, Homebrew lives at `/usr/local/bin` instead — you can keep both in the PATH list as shown.

The reference for the Desktop config format is exactly the page you linked — `command` + `args` + optional `env` is the whole shape.

Couple things to verify — that file mixing `preferences` keys with `mcpServers` looks suspicious. The keys `coworkScheduledTasksEnabled`, `sidebarMode: "epitaxy"`, etc. aren't standard Claude Desktop preferences and suggest you may be editing **the wrong file**.

The official path is exactly:

```
~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json
```

Run these to diagnose:

## 1. Confirm which files exist

```bash
ls -la ~/Library/Application\ Support/Claude/
```

You should see `claude_desktop_config.json`. If you see something else like `config.json`, `preferences.json`, or `settings.json` mixed in — those are different files and `mcpServers` belongs only in `claude_desktop_config.json`.

## 2. Validate the JSON (a typo silently disables the whole config)

```bash
python3 -m json.tool ~/Library/Application\ Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json
```

If it errors, the JSON is malformed and Claude Desktop ignored the file entirely.

## 3. Check the Claude Desktop MCP logs

This is where Claude tells you why it failed to start your server:

```bash
ls ~/Library/Logs/Claude/
tail -50 ~/Library/Logs/Claude/mcp*.log 2>/dev/null
```

Look for lines mentioning `phone-controll`. Common causes you'll see there: `command not found`, `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'mcp_phone_controll'`, or JSON parse errors.

## 4. Smoke-test the exact command Claude Desktop will spawn

```bash
/Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll/.venv/bin/python -m mcp_phone_controll < /dev/null
```

It should hang waiting for stdin (no error output). Press Ctrl-C. If you get an `ImportError` or `command not found`, that's why Claude can't start it.

## 5. The minimal config that should work

Replace your `claude_desktop_config.json` with **exactly** this — strip the `preferences` block in case that file shape is what's confusing the parser:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "phone-controll": {
      "command": "/Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll/.venv/bin/python",
      "args": ["-m", "mcp_phone_controll"],
      "env": {
        "PATH": "/opt/homebrew/bin:/opt/homebrew/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

If those `preferences` keys belong to a separate file, this won't break anything. If your screenshot is from the new Claude Desktop "Cowork" build, the config path may be different — paste the output of step 1 and I'll point you at the right file.

After saving: **fully quit Claude Desktop with ⌘Q** (closing the window isn't enough) and reopen.

Paste the outputs of steps 1, 2, and 3 and we'll have the answer in one round.

content.text:
Mystery solved — you're running **Claude Cowork**, not standard Claude Desktop. Tell-tale signs from your dump:

- Logs include `cowork_vm_node.log`, `cowork_vm_swift.log`, `coworkd.log`
- Config keys `coworkScheduledTasksEnabled`, `sidebarMode: "epitaxy"`, `chicagoEnabled`
- Files `bridge-state.json`, `cowork-enabled-cli-ops.json`
- Directories `claude-code-vm`, `vm_bundles`

And critically: **no `mcp*.log` exists** → the app never tried to spawn your server. That means it's not reading `claude_desktop_config.json` for MCP registration. Cowork is a different app with a different config layout.

Notice you also have a separate `config.json` (3016 bytes) sitting next to `claude_desktop_config.json` (570 bytes). Cowork likely reads MCP servers from `config.json`.

## Find the truth in two steps

```bash
cat ~/Library/Application\ Support/Claude/config.json
```

Paste the output (redact tokens/IDs if any). If it has a `"mcpServers"` block, that's where your entry goes.

Also — in the Settings UI from your screenshot, **click "Edit Config" again** and check the editor's title bar / file path. That button opens whichever file the app actually reads. If it opens `config.json` (not `claude_desktop_config.json`), that confirms the layout.

## In the meantime — guaranteed-working fallback: Claude Code

You already have Claude Code installed (`~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude-code/` exists). Claude Code's MCP integration is rock-solid and uses the standard format. From any terminal:

```bash
claude mcp add phone-controll \
  /Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll/.venv/bin/python \
  -m mcp_phone_controll
```

Then verify:

```bash
claude mcp list
```

You'll see `phone-controll` with a connected status. Open `claude` (or `claude` in any project dir) and the MCP is live for that session — same tools, same `list_devices`, `take_screenshot`, etc.

This is also what's used in actual development workflows on your projects (`claude` from inside your Flutter repo). It's arguably the right home for the MCP given you do Flutter work in Claude Code anyway.

## Once we see config.json

I can tell you the exact JSON shape Cowork wants. The block name might be `mcpServers` (same shape) or something Cowork-specific. Paste the contents and we'll have it wired in one round.

content.text:
Two things:

## Claude Code is wired and ready

Look at your `claude mcp list` output:

```
phone-controll: /Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll/.venv/bin/python -m mcp_phone_controll - ✓ Connected
```

That's done. You can use the MCP **right now** from any Claude Code session — open `claude` in any directory (e.g. inside your Flutter project) and Claude can drive your phone immediately. This is the surface where it's most useful anyway, since on-device testing happens during development inside your repos.

(The `-m` error was just a CLI parsing issue on the new `add` invocation — earlier successful registration is still active. If you ever need to re-register, the form that works is `claude mcp add phone-controll -- <command> <args>` with the `--` separator before the command.)

## Cowork — `config.json` has no mcpServers block

Your `config.json` is purely OAuth tokens + UI preferences (`locale`, `userThemeMode`, `dxt:allowlist*`). No MCP-related keys. So Cowork **isn't** using `config.json` for MCP — it's the same `claude_desktop_config.json` you already edited.

Two remaining possibilities for why the UI shows "No servers added":

1. **Restart wasn't full.** ⌘W closes the window but the app keeps running. You need ⌘Q (or right-click the dock icon → Quit), then relaunch. Confirm it was a hard restart by running:
   ```bash
   pgrep -fl Claude
   ```
   If that prints anything before you reopen, kill it: `pkill -f Claude`.

2. **Cowork's local MCP support may not be live yet on your build.** Cowork is a beta variant ("Chicago"/"epitaxy" feature flags in your config). Local stdio MCPs are a Claude Desktop Stable feature; the experimental cowork build may currently only support remote HTTP MCPs (the three Google ones in `claude mcp list` are HTTP). The "Edit Config" button could exist for parity with Stable while the runtime hasn't been wired through.

### Quick way to test theory #2

After a hard restart, check the logs for any reference to your server:

```bash
grep -i "phone-controll\|mcp_phone_controll\|mcpServers" ~/Library/Logs/Claude/*.log 2>/dev/null
```

- If you see startup attempts → it's a config/path issue (paste any errors).
- If you see **nothing** → the runtime simply isn't reading the `mcpServers` block in this build. Wait for an update or use Claude Code in the meantime.

## My recommendation

Use Claude Code for this MCP. It's:

- Already working (`✓ Connected`).
- The right surface for on-device testing — you're going to want it in your project terminal alongside your Flutter code anyway.
- Inheriting the testing skill we wrote (skills auto-load there).

When you're inside a Flutter project and want Claude to drive your phone, just type `claude` and try the smoke prompt I gave you. The Cowork question can wait until either it gets the feature or you switch to Claude Desktop Stable.

Two things:

## 1. You don't need to re-register

The earlier registration is live — your current Claude Code session has all 28 `phone-controll` tools loaded right now. I can see them. Let me prove it by actually calling one:
{"ok": false, "error": {"code": "UnexpectedFailure", "message": "FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'pymobiledevice3'", "details": {}}}

content.text:
Two issues exposed by that one call:

1. **CLI syntax** for `claude mcp add` needs `--` to separate the wrapper's flags from the command's flags.
2. **Real bug:** when Claude Code spawns the MCP, it inherits a minimal PATH that doesn't include `/opt/homebrew/bin` or the venv's `bin/`. `adb` worked, but `pymobiledevice3` (which lives only inside the venv at `.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3`) couldn't be found, and the `FileNotFoundError` wasn't caught — it bubbled up as `UnexpectedFailure`. Both should be fixed in the code so this works regardless of who spawns it.

Patching both: (a) make `PyMobileDevice3Cli` default to the venv-relative binary, and (b) make `AsyncProcessRunner` translate "binary missing" into a clean typed failure instead of an uncaught exception.

async def run(
        self,
        argv: list[str],
        cwd: Path | None = None,
        timeout_s: float | None = None,
        env: dict[str, str] | None = None,
    ) -> ProcessResult:
        proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
            *argv,
            cwd=str(cwd) if cwd else None,
            env=env,
            stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
            stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        )
        try:
            stdout_b, stderr_b = await asyncio.wait_for(proc.communicate(), timeout=timeout_s)
        except asyncio.TimeoutError:
            proc.kill()
            await proc.wait()
            raise
        return ProcessResult(
            returncode=proc.returncode if proc.returncode is not None else -1,
            stdout=stdout_b.decode("utf-8", errors="replace"),
            stderr=stderr_b.decode("utf-8", errors="replace"),
        )

toolUseResult.originalFile:
"""Single async-subprocess chokepoint. Every shell-out goes through here."""

from __future__ import annotations

import asyncio
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Protocol


@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
class ProcessResult:
    returncode: int
    stdout: str
    stderr: str

    @property
    def ok(self) -> bool:
        return self.returncode == 0


class ProcessRunner(Protocol):
    async def run(
        self,
        argv: list[str],
        cwd: Path | None = None,
        timeout_s: float | None = None,
        env: dict[str, str] | None = None,
    ) -> ProcessResult: ...

    async def stream(
        self,
        argv: list[str],
        cwd: Path | None = None,
        env: dict[str, str] | None = None,
    ) -> "asyncio.subprocess.Process": ...


class AsyncProcessRunner:
    """Concrete asyncio-based runner. Decouples repos from the asyncio API."""

    async def run(
        self,
        argv: list[str],
        cwd: Path | None = None,
        timeout_s: float | None = None,
        env: dict[str, str] | None = None,
    ) -> ProcessResult:
        proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
            *argv,
            cwd=str(cwd) if cwd else None,
            env=env,
            stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
            stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        )
        try:
            stdout_b, stderr_b = await asyncio.wait_for(proc.communicate(), timeout=timeout_s)
        except asyncio.TimeoutError:
            proc.kill()
            await proc.wait()
            raise
        return ProcessResult(
            returncode=proc.returncode if proc.returncode is not None else -1,
            stdout=stdout_b.decode("utf-8", errors="replace"),
            stderr=stderr_b.decode("utf-8", errors="replace"),
        )

    async def stream(
        self,
        argv: list[str],
        cwd: Path | None = None,
        env: dict[str, str] | None = None,
    ) -> asyncio.subprocess.Process:
        return await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
            *argv,
            cwd=str(cwd) if cwd else None,
            env=env,
            stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
            stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        )

from __future__ import annotations

import shutil
import sys
from pathlib import Path

from .process_runner import ProcessResult, ProcessRunner


def _default_pymobiledevice3_path() -> str:
    """Prefer the venv-installed binary so MCP host PATH doesn't matter.

    pymobiledevice3 ships as a Python entry point in the same venv as our own
    process, so `<sys.executable>/../pymobiledevice3` is the most reliable path.
    Falls back to whatever `shutil.which` finds, then to the bare name.
    """
    venv_candidate = Path(sys.executable).parent / "pymobiledevice3"
    if venv_candidate.exists():
        return str(venv_candidate)
    found = shutil.which("pymobiledevice3")
    return found or "pymobiledevice3"


class PyMobileDevice3Cli:
    def __init__(self, runner: ProcessRunner, binary: str | None = None) -> None:
        self._runner = runner
        self._bin = binary or _default_pymobiledevice3_path()

"""Async wrapper around the `flutter` CLI."""

from __future__ import annotations

from pathlib import Path

from .process_runner import ProcessResult, ProcessRunner


class FlutterCli:
    def __init__(self, runner: ProcessRunner, flutter_path: str = "flutter") -> None:
        self._runner = runner
        self._flutter = flutter_path

    async def build_apk(
        self,
        project_path: Path,
        mode: str = "debug",
        flavor: str | None = None,
        timeout_s: float = 600.0,
    ) -> ProcessResult:
        argv = [self._flutter, "build", "apk", f"--{mode}"]
        if flavor:
            argv += ["--flavor", flavor]
        return await self._runner.run(argv, cwd=project_path, timeout_s=timeout_s)

    async def build_ipa(
        self,
        project_path: Path,
        mode: str = "debug",
        flavor: str | None = None,
        timeout_s: float = 1200.0,
    ) -> ProcessResult:
        argv = [self._flutter, "build", "ipa", f"--{mode}"]
        if flavor:
            argv += ["--flavor", flavor]
        return await self._runner.run(argv, cwd=project_path, timeout_s=timeout_s)

    async def test_unit(
        self, project_path: Path, timeout_s: float = 600.0
    ) -> ProcessResult:
        return await self._runner.run(
            [self._flutter, "test", "--reporter=json"],
            cwd=project_path,
            timeout_s=timeout_s,
        )

    async def test_integration(
        self,
        project_path: Path,
        device_serial: str,
        test_path: str = "integration_test/",
        timeout_s: float = 1800.0,
    ) -> ProcessResult:
        return await self._runner.run(
            [
                self._flutter,
                "test",
                test_path,
                "-d",
                device_serial,
                "--reporter=json",
            ],
            cwd=project_path,
            timeout_s=timeout_s,
        )

44/44 still green. Now your two questions:

## On the CLI syntax

You don't need to re-register (it's already connected, your current session sees all 28 tools). But for next time, the form that works is:

```bash
claude mcp add phone-controll -- /Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll/.venv/bin/python -m mcp_phone_controll
```

The `--` is what separates `claude mcp add`'s own flags from the spawned command's args. Without it, `-m` looks like a flag for `claude` itself.

## On picking up the patch

The MCP server runs as a long-lived subprocess of Claude Code. Code changes only take effect after you restart it. **In your Claude Code app**: open the slash-command palette and run `/mcp` then `Reconnect` for `phone-controll`, or just restart the session entirely (close the conversation tab and start a new one). Then tell me to call `list_devices` again and we'll see your phones come back through Claude this time, end-to-end.

Once I see your Galaxy in the response inside this conversation, you're 100% live and can move on to actually using it.

content.text:
The MCP server is a subprocess Claude Code spawned earlier — it's still running the **old** code. To pick up the patch, restart it. Two options:

## Option A — restart the whole Claude Code session (simplest)

End this conversation, start a new one in any directory. Claude Code will spawn a fresh MCP process on first tool call, with the new code.

## Option B — reconnect just the MCP from inside this session

Type `/mcp` in your input box. A panel opens listing servers. Pick `phone-controll` and choose **Reconnect** (or **Restart**). The server respawns with the patched code, this conversation keeps its history.

## Then just tell me to use it

Once you've done either, send a message like:

> Use the phone-controll MCP. Call `list_devices` and show me the result. If the Android device is there, also call `select_device` for it, then `take_screenshot` and tell me where the file landed.

I'll call the tools live in this conversation. You'll see the JSON envelopes inline. With the patches in place, the previous `pymobiledevice3 not found` error should now show up as a clean per-iOS-tool failure instead of breaking `list_devices` entirely — and your Galaxy should come back fine.

That round-trip — me calling a tool, you seeing my Claude actually drive your phone — is the definitive proof the integration works. After that, you can give it real work.
