toolUseResult.originalFile:
# mcp-phone-controll

A local MCP server that lets Claude Code build, deploy, and exercise Flutter apps on real Android **and** iOS devices through a single uniform tool surface.

## What it does

Same MCP tool names work for both platforms — Claude calls `install_app`, `tap_text`, `read_logs`, `run_integration_tests`, etc. The server figures out which device a serial belongs to and routes to the right backend.

- discover devices over USB (ADB for Android, usbmuxd for iOS)
- build a Flutter project for either platform (`flutter build apk` / `flutter build ipa`)
- install / launch / stop / clear / uninstall apps
- drive the UI: tap, swipe, type, find by text/resource-id, wait, dump tree, assert visible
- observe: screenshot, screen record (Android only), logcat / os_log
- run `flutter test` and `flutter test integration_test/` and report structured results
- group artefacts (screenshots, logs, recordings) per session

## Architecture

Clean Architecture in three layers with a presentation shell:

```
domain/         pure business logic — entities, Result, Failures, repository protocols, use cases
infrastructure/ outbound adapters — adb, flutter CLI, uiautomator2, pymobiledevice3, WebDriverAgent
data/           parsers + repository implementations
data/repositories/composite/   platform-routing composites — same Protocols, route by serial
presentation/   MCP server — tool registry maps tool names to use cases
container.py    composition root — wires every concrete repo behind composites
```

Dependency direction is inward: presentation → domain ← data → infrastructure. Domain knows nothing about ADB, MCP, the filesystem, or any platform.

Errors are returned, not thrown. Every use case returns `Result[T]` (`Ok | Err`) with a typed `Failure`. The MCP layer translates that into a uniform `{ok, data, error}` envelope.

### How the platform routing works

`CachingPlatformResolver` maintains a `serial → Platform` cache. `CompositeDeviceRepository.list_devices()` queries both Android and iOS backends and populates the cache. Every other composite (`CompositeLifecycleRepository`, `CompositeUiRepository`, …) delegates per call by looking up the serial in the resolver.

Use cases never see a `Platform` argument (except `build_app`, which has no serial to resolve from) — they just call the protocol they were given.

## Setup

External prerequisites:

**Android:**
- `adb` on `PATH` (Android Platform Tools)
- USB debugging enabled, host fingerprint authorised
- One-time per device: `python -m uiautomator2 init`

**iOS:**
- macOS host, Xcode + command-line tools installed
- `pymobiledevice3` reachable on `PATH` (installed via the package's deps)
- iOS device trusted to host
- For app launch / on-device tests: Developer Disk Image mounted (`pymobiledevice3 mounter auto-mount`)
- For UI automation: WebDriverAgent built and installed on the device (one-time, via Xcode)
- Code signing configured in the Flutter project (standard iOS dev requirement)

**Both:**
- `flutter` on `PATH`

Install:

```bash
uv venv --python 3.11
source .venv/bin/activate
uv pip install -e ".[dev]"
```

Run tests (no phone needed — everything below the repository boundary is faked):

```bash
pytest
```

Register with Claude Code:

```bash
claude mcp add phone-controll -- python -m mcp_phone_controll
```

## Tools (28 total)

All tools are uniform across Android and iOS. Where a tool isn't supported on a platform (e.g. screen recording on iOS, `clear_data` / `grant_permission` on iOS), the call returns a typed `Failure` with a hint.

`build_app` and `install_app` accept an optional `platform` parameter. `install_app` defaults to the selected device's platform.

## Layout

```
src/mcp_phone_controll/
  domain/
    result.py / failures.py / entities.py / repositories.py
    usecases/                  one class per action, extending BaseUseCase
  infrastructure/
    process_runner.py          single async-subprocess chokepoint
    adb_client.py              Android wrapper
    flutter_cli.py             flutter CLI (build apk + build ipa + test)
    uiautomator2_factory.py    Android UI client cache
    pymobiledevice3_cli.py     iOS device CLI wrapper
    wda_factory.py             iOS UI client (facebook-wda over usbmux)
  data/
    parsers/                   adb-devices / logcat / pymobiledevice3 usbmux / flutter test JSON
    repositories/
      adb_*.py                 Android implementations
      ios_*.py / wda_*.py      iOS implementations
      flutter_*.py             cross-platform Flutter (build, test)
      composite/               platform-routing composites
  presentation/
    serialization.py / tool_registry.py / mcp_server.py
  container.py                 composition root
  __main__.py                  entrypoint
```

## Testing strategy

- Use cases are tested against fake repositories — no subprocess, no phone.
- Parsers (adb-devices, logcat, flutter JSON reporter, pymobiledevice3 usbmux) are pure functions with fixture tests.
- Composite routing is tested with fakes: confirms platform resolution, per-serial dispatch, and that an iOS toolchain failure does not break the Android path.
- The tool registry is tested end-to-end with fake use cases to verify schema/dispatch.
- Real ADB / uiautomator2 / pymobiledevice3 / WDA repositories are exercised manually with a phone.

`pytest` runs the full 44-test suite in under a second.

## What's deferred

- iOS screen recording (requires `xrecord` or QuickTime — not exposed by pymobiledevice3).
- True streaming of `pymobiledevice3 syslog live` — currently approximated via a windowed `syslog collect`.
- Wi-Fi ADB, emulator parallel runs, network conditioning, signing/release flows.

content.text: Update the testing skill to be Patrol-first:

input.content:
---
name: mcp-phone-controll-testing
description: On-device testing methodology for the mcp-phone-controll MCP server. Patrol-first for Flutter (locale-independent, widget-Key-driven). Use when planning, executing, or reviewing tests on a real Android or iOS device through the MCP — including AR, Vision, ML Kit, and other native-plugin flows. Provides a Test Validity Rubric to decide which test cases are valid for the requirement and which need to be rewritten before they're worth running, plus a failure-triage cheat sheet keyed to the MCP's Failure codes. Use also when the user asks to verify acceptance criteria on hardware, build a regression suite, audit an existing test plan, or set up a new app for on-device testing.
---

# On-Device Testing — mcp-phone-controll (Patrol-first)

## Purpose

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      WHY THIS SKILL EXISTS                                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│  The MCP gives Claude superpowers on a real phone. Without a    │
│  methodology those superpowers produce noisy, flaky, locale-    │
│  coupled tests. This skill keeps every on-device run tied to:   │
│                                                                 │
│   1. A specific requirement / acceptance criterion              │
│   2. A test case that meets the Validity Rubric                 │
│   3. The right framework — Patrol for Flutter app UI, raw       │
│      UI tools for system chrome only                            │
│   4. An MCP tool sequence that produces evidence Claude can     │
│      cite (TestRun, screenshot path, log line)                  │
│   5. A failure-triage path keyed to typed Failure codes         │
│   6. A cross-platform parity check when the feature ships on    │
│      both Android and iOS                                       │
│                                                                 │
│  If a test case can't pass the Rubric, REWRITE IT before        │
│  running. A red test that proves nothing is worse than no test. │
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## When to use

Trigger this skill when the user is:

- planning a test run that will hit a real device through `mcp-phone-controll`
- writing or reviewing `integration_test/` Patrol tests
- auditing an existing test plan ("are these still valid?")
- triaging a failed `run_patrol_test` / `run_integration_tests` result
- adding cross-platform coverage (Android-tested feature now needs iOS sign-off)
- building a regression checklist for release sign-off
- setting up a new app for on-device testing for the first time

Skip this skill for:

- Pure unit tests (use `flutter-testing` — no device needed).
- Widget tests that run via `flutter test`.
- One-off "click around and see" exploration where evidence isn't required.

## The Pre-flight (do this BEFORE any test work)

ALWAYS open with these two MCP calls:

1. **`check_environment`** — confirms `adb`, `flutter`, `patrol`, `pymobiledevice3` are all green. If any check is `ok: false`, stop and run the suggested `fix` command. Do NOT proceed past a red doctor check.
2. **`inspect_project`** with the user's project path — confirms it's a Flutter project and reports `test_frameworks`. If `PATROL` is in the list, all Flutter UI tests go through Patrol. If only `FLUTTER` is listed, recommend the user add `patrol` and `patrol_cli` to `pubspec.yaml` before writing tests for app UI.

These two calls eliminate ~80% of the "ran the tests but they fail for environment reasons" debugging.

## Framework decision tree

```
What are we testing?
├─ Flutter app UI (your code, your widgets)
│  └─ Patrol            ← run_patrol_test / run_patrol_suite
│     • Drive by Key, not by visible text
│     • Native dialogs (permissions, ATT) handled inside the same test
│     • Locale-independent
│
├─ System UI (Settings, OS dialogs, ATT prompt before app starts)
│  └─ Raw UI tools      ← tap_text + find_element
│     • OK to use display text BUT pass the localized string
│     • Localized strings should come from the test plan, not be hardcoded
│
├─ Build / install / launch / clear (no UI driving)
│  └─ Lifecycle tools   ← install_app, launch_app, clear_app_data
│
├─ Logs / screenshots / video
│  └─ Observation tools ← read_logs, tail_logs, take_screenshot, start_recording
│
├─ AR / Vision / ML Kit (Flutter-side)
│  └─ Patrol drives UI; tail_logs watches the session telemetry; screenshots
│     capture placement at known steps. Don't pixel-assert AR overlays —
│     assert log markers (e.g. tracking state) and capture for human review.
│
└─ Native iOS / native Android / RN / web
   └─ NOT YET — the architecture supports adding XCUITest, Espresso, Detox,
      Playwright as new frameworks (see README "Adding a new test framework").
      Until then, treat as out of scope.
```

## The Test Validity Rubric

Every on-device test case must pass **all** of these before it's worth running. If any item is "no", REWRITE the case (or split it).

| # | Criterion             | Concrete check                                                              |
|---|-----------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Mapped to AC**      | The case names the exact acceptance criterion or requirement ID it covers. |
| 2 | **Single behaviour**  | One `Given/When/Then`. Multiple `When`s = split.                            |
| 3 | **Deterministic**     | No reliance on real time, network, randomness, or device defaults.          |
| 4 | **Observable**        | The "Then" is verifiable by Patrol's `expect`, `read_logs` regex, or a `TestRun` field — not just "looks right". |
| 5 | **Isolated**          | Runs after `clear_app_data` (Android) or `uninstall + install` (iOS). No carry-over. |
| 6 | **Idempotent setup**  | Re-running from scratch yields identical evidence.                          |
| 7 | **Bounded waits**     | Every wait has a numeric timeout. No `Future.delayed`-style fallbacks.      |
| 8 | **Locale-independent**| Uses **widget Keys** for app UI (`Key('signInButton')`), not display text.  |
| 9 | **Failure-aware**     | The case states what failure mode it would catch.                           |
| 10| **Cheap diagnosis**   | On failure, captures `screenshot` + relevant `read_logs` so triage doesn't need a re-run. |

**Rewrite signals** — if you see any of these, the test case is invalid, not the code:

- "Test passes locally, fails in CI" with no further detail → criterion 3.
- Uses `await tester.pump(Duration(seconds: 5))` to wait for UI → criterion 7.
- Asserts `find.byType(Container)` count → criterion 4 (structure, not behaviour).
- Hardcodes `"Sign in"`, `"Settings"`, `"Allow"` → **criterion 8**, your test will break the moment the OS or app is in a different language. Use `Key('signInButton')`. For system dialogs, parametrise the localized string in the test plan and pass it in.
- Depends on a previous test having logged in → criterion 5.
- "It's just a smoke test" with no AC mapped → criterion 1.

## Requirement → Test Traceability

Before adding tests, build (or have the user supply) a 3-column matrix:

| AC ID | Acceptance criterion (one sentence)                              | Test case ID(s) |
|-------|------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------|
| AC-1  | A signed-in user sees their email on the home screen.            | TC-HOME-01      |
| AC-2  | Tapping "Sign out" returns to the login screen within 2s.        | TC-AUTH-07      |

Rules:
- Every AC needs **≥ 1** test case. Flag gaps explicitly.
- Every test case needs **exactly 1** AC. Multi-AC cases are too coarse.
- ACs without a clear "Then" observable through Patrol or MCP tools are **specification bugs** — flag them, don't paper over them.

## Test pyramid

```
                        ┌──────────────┐
                        │  smoke (5)   │  ← golden flow on real hardware
                        │              │     run on every build
                        └──────────────┘
                     ┌──────────────────┐
                     │ patrol (~30)     │  ← run_patrol_suite on hardware
                     │  per-feature     │     before release
                     └──────────────────┘
                ┌──────────────────────────┐
                │     widget (~hundreds)   │  ← flutter test — no MCP
                └──────────────────────────┘
            ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
            │       unit (~thousands)          │  ← flutter test — no MCP
            └──────────────────────────────────┘
```

If a unit-level concern is being verified at integration level, that's a **misplaced test** — recommend moving it down. Conversely, if a hardware-only concern (real ATT prompt, real biometric, real ARKit session) is being mocked at widget level, recommend moving it up.

## MCP loop patterns

### A) New session preflight (always do this)

```
check_environment                                  # confirm toolchain
inspect_project(project_path=...)                  # confirm Patrol available
list_devices                                       # confirm device visible
select_device(serial=...)
new_session(label="<feature>-<sha>")
```

### B) Smoke check (verify build + launch, no UI assertions)

```
install_app(project_path=..., mode="debug")
launch_app(package_id=...)
take_screenshot(label="post-launch")
read_logs(min_level="W", since_s=10)               # assert no FATAL/E in app's tag
```

### C) Single-AC integration case via Patrol

```
list_patrol_tests(project_path=...)                # discover what's available
run_patrol_test(project_path=..., test_path="integration_test/auth_test.dart")
take_screenshot(label="ac-<id>")                   # for the report
```

The Patrol test itself does the asserting via `expect(...)` against widget Keys.

### D) Suite run (release sign-off)

```
new_session(label="release-<sha>")
list_devices / select_device
run_patrol_suite(project_path=..., build_mode="profile")
```

Inspect the returned `TestRun`. Map failures to AC IDs in the report. If `errored > 0` (not `failed`), treat as infrastructure (criterion 3) and re-run before filing.

### E) AR / Vision / Camera flow

```
clear_app_data(package_id=...)                     # Android — fresh permissions
launch_app(package_id=...)
# Patrol test handles the system camera-permission dialog inside the test
run_patrol_test(test_path="integration_test/ar_anchor_test.dart")
# AR-specific evidence:
take_screenshot(label="anchor-placed")
read_logs(tag="ARSession", min_level="I")          # tracking state, anchor IDs
```

For long-running async events (AR session converges, ML Kit returns a result):

```
tail_logs(until_pattern="trackingState=normal", tag="ARSession", timeout_s=30)
```

### F) System UI (rare — only when Patrol can't reach it)

```
press_key("home")
launch_app(package_id="com.android.settings")
# Localized lookup — DO NOT hardcode English
tap_text(text=user_supplied_localized_label)        # "Ustawienia" on Polish phone
```

## Failure-triage cheat sheet

| Code                        | Most likely cause                                  | Next MCP tool                                    |
|-----------------------------|----------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| Any check fails in `check_environment` | Toolchain missing / wrong version          | Run the `fix` command from the report.            |
| `NoDeviceSelectedFailure`   | Skipped `select_device`.                           | `list_devices` → `select_device`.                |
| `DeviceNotFoundFailure`     | Cable popped, device locked, or never listed.      | `list_devices`. If absent, ask user.             |
| `AdbFailure`                | adb non-zero exit. Read `details.stderr`.          | If `unauthorized` → re-trust. If offline → reconnect. |
| `BuildFailure`              | `flutter build` failed.                            | Stop. Read `stderr`. Don't run more tests.       |
| `InstallFailure`            | APK signature mismatch / older version installed.  | `uninstall_app` then retry `install_app`.        |
| `LaunchFailure` (iOS)       | DDI not mounted, Developer Mode off, tunneld dead. | Run `check_environment` again — it'll tell you which. |
| `UiElementNotFoundFailure`  | Selector wrong, screen changed, app crashed.       | `take_screenshot` + `dump_ui` + `read_logs`.     |
| `TimeoutFailure`            | Wait was tight, or the trigger never fired.        | Increase once. Still failing → real bug.         |
| `UiFailure`                 | UIA2 / WDA disconnected.                           | `list_devices` again (forces reconnect).          |
| `TestExecutionFailure`      | `flutter`/`patrol` test produced no JSON events.   | Read `details.stderr`. Often a missing dep.      |
| `FilesystemFailure`         | Artifact dir not writable.                         | `new_session` again or check `~/.mcp_phone_controll/`. |
| `InvalidArgumentFailure`    | Bad/missing tool argument.                         | Re-read schema; do not retry blindly.            |
| `UnexpectedFailure`         | Bug in the MCP server itself.                      | Capture, surface to user, do not retry.          |

## Cross-platform parity protocol

When a feature must work on both Android and iOS, run **the same Patrol test** against each device. Patrol's widget-Key-driven approach means the same `_test.dart` file is the test for both platforms.

Parity checklist:
1. The Patrol test passes on both serials in the same `new_session`.
2. Screenshots show the equivalent screen at the equivalent step.
3. `read_logs` shows the same business-event tags on both platforms.
4. iOS `os_version` differences are noted in the report.
5. Tools unsupported on iOS (`clear_app_data`, `grant_permission`, `start_recording`) have a documented workaround in the test plan — never silently skipped.

## Reporting

Every test session ends with a structured report:

```
SESSION:    <session_id>            ← from new_session
DEVICE:     <serial> (<platform> <os_version>)
FRAMEWORK:  patrol | flutter
ARTIFACTS:  <path>                  ← from get_artifacts_dir

PASSED:    <n>
FAILED:    <n>
ERRORED:   <n>
SKIPPED:   <n>  (reasons must be listed)

AC COVERAGE
  AC-1  ✓  TC-HOME-01
  AC-2  ✗  TC-AUTH-07     →  see screenshots/auth-fail.png + logs/auth-fail.log
  AC-3  ⚠  no test         →  GAP

REWRITES NEEDED
  TC-XYZ-04 — Rubric criterion 8 (hardcodes English "Sign in"; switch to Key)
  TC-XYZ-09 — Rubric criterion 1 (no AC mapped)

TRIAGE
  TC-AUTH-07 → Patrol expect failed: widget with Key('homeFeed') not found
              → screenshot shows network error toast
              → read_logs: "AuthRepo: 401 from /me"
              → Likely real bug, not test-side.
```

The report is the deliverable. Without it, "ran the tests on a phone" is not evidence anyone can act on.

## Quick decision tree

```
Is this an AC or a hunch?
  hunch  → don't write a hardware test; explore manually first
  AC     ↓

Can a unit/widget test prove it?
  yes    → push it down the pyramid; don't use the MCP
  no     ↓

check_environment + inspect_project → all green?
  no     → run fixes; STOP until green
  yes    ↓

Does it pass all 10 Rubric criteria?
  no     → REWRITE before running
  yes    ↓

Pick MCP loop A→F (most cases will be A then C or D)
Run on Android serial via Patrol
Cross-platform feature?
  yes    → run identical test on iOS serial; compare evidence
  no     ↓

Produce the SESSION report. Map every result to its AC.
```

## What this skill will NOT do

- Decide whether a feature ships. The skill produces evidence; the human owns the call.
- Pretend a flaky test is "intermittent". Flake = Rubric criterion 3 violation = rewrite.
- Run destructive operations (factory reset, `uninstall` on prod-data apps) without explicit user confirmation.
- Silently mock anything for a "hardware" run. If a hardware test needs a mock, the test is misplaced.
- Write hardcoded display text into tests. If the user asks for `tap_text("Sign in")` on app UI, push back: "use a Key — that test will break in Polish."

---
name: mcp-phone-controll-testing
description: On-device testing methodology for the mcp-phone-controll MCP server. Patrol-first for Flutter (locale-independent, widget-Key-driven). Use when planning, executing, or reviewing tests on a real Android or iOS device through the MCP — including AR, Vision, ML Kit, and other native-plugin flows. Provides a Test Validity Rubric to decide which test cases are valid for the requirement and which need to be rewritten before they're worth running, plus a failure-triage cheat sheet keyed to the MCP's Failure codes. Use also when the user asks to verify acceptance criteria on hardware, build a regression suite, audit an existing test plan, or set up a new app for on-device testing.
---

# On-Device Testing — mcp-phone-controll (Patrol-first)

## Purpose

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│      WHY THIS SKILL EXISTS                                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│  The MCP gives Claude superpowers on a real phone. Without a    │
│  methodology those superpowers produce noisy, flaky, locale-    │
│  coupled tests. This skill keeps every on-device run tied to:   │
│                                                                 │
│   1. A specific requirement / acceptance criterion              │
│   2. A test case that meets the Validity Rubric                 │
│   3. The right framework — Patrol for Flutter app UI, raw       │
│      UI tools for system chrome only                            │
│   4. An MCP tool sequence that produces evidence Claude can     │
│      cite (TestRun, screenshot path, log line)                  │
│   5. A failure-triage path keyed to typed Failure codes         │
│   6. A cross-platform parity check when the feature ships on    │
│      both Android and iOS                                       │
│                                                                 │
│  If a test case can't pass the Rubric, REWRITE IT before        │
│  running. A red test that proves nothing is worse than no test. │
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## When to use

Trigger this skill when the user is:

- planning a test run that will hit a real device through `mcp-phone-controll`
- writing or reviewing `integration_test/` Patrol tests
- auditing an existing test plan ("are these still valid?")
- triaging a failed `run_patrol_test` / `run_integration_tests` result
- adding cross-platform coverage (Android-tested feature now needs iOS sign-off)
- building a regression checklist for release sign-off
- setting up a new app for on-device testing for the first time

Skip this skill for:

- Pure unit tests (use `flutter-testing` — no device needed).
- Widget tests that run via `flutter test`.
- One-off "click around and see" exploration where evidence isn't required.

## The Pre-flight (do this BEFORE any test work)

ALWAYS open with these two MCP calls:

1. **`check_environment`** — confirms `adb`, `flutter`, `patrol`, `pymobiledevice3` are all green. If any check is `ok: false`, stop and run the suggested `fix` command. Do NOT proceed past a red doctor check.
2. **`inspect_project`** with the user's project path — confirms it's a Flutter project and reports `test_frameworks`. If `PATROL` is in the list, all Flutter UI tests go through Patrol. If only `FLUTTER` is listed, recommend the user add `patrol` and `patrol_cli` to `pubspec.yaml` before writing tests for app UI.

These two calls eliminate ~80% of the "ran the tests but they fail for environment reasons" debugging.

## Framework decision tree

```
What are we testing?
├─ Flutter app UI (your code, your widgets)
│  └─ Patrol            ← run_patrol_test / run_patrol_suite
│     • Drive by Key, not by visible text
│     • Native dialogs (permissions, ATT) handled inside the same test
│     • Locale-independent
│
├─ System UI (Settings, OS dialogs, ATT prompt before app starts)
│  └─ Raw UI tools      ← tap_text + find_element
│     • OK to use display text BUT pass the localized string
│     • Localized strings should come from the test plan, not be hardcoded
│
├─ Build / install / launch / clear (no UI driving)
│  └─ Lifecycle tools   ← install_app, launch_app, clear_app_data
│
├─ Logs / screenshots / video
│  └─ Observation tools ← read_logs, tail_logs, take_screenshot, start_recording
│
├─ AR / Vision / ML Kit (Flutter-side)
│  └─ Patrol drives UI; tail_logs watches the session telemetry; screenshots
│     capture placement at known steps. Don't pixel-assert AR overlays —
│     assert log markers (e.g. tracking state) and capture for human review.
│
└─ Native iOS / native Android / RN / web
   └─ NOT YET — the architecture supports adding XCUITest, Espresso, Detox,
      Playwright as new frameworks (see README "Adding a new test framework").
      Until then, treat as out of scope.
```

## The Test Validity Rubric

Every on-device test case must pass **all** of these before it's worth running. If any item is "no", REWRITE the case (or split it).

| # | Criterion             | Concrete check                                                              |
|---|-----------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Mapped to AC**      | The case names the exact acceptance criterion or requirement ID it covers. |
| 2 | **Single behaviour**  | One `Given/When/Then`. Multiple `When`s = split.                            |
| 3 | **Deterministic**     | No reliance on real time, network, randomness, or device defaults.          |
| 4 | **Observable**        | The "Then" is verifiable by Patrol's `expect`, `read_logs` regex, or a `TestRun` field — not just "looks right". |
| 5 | **Isolated**          | Runs after `clear_app_data` (Android) or `uninstall + install` (iOS). No carry-over. |
| 6 | **Idempotent setup**  | Re-running from scratch yields identical evidence.                          |
| 7 | **Bounded waits**     | Every wait has a numeric timeout. No `Future.delayed`-style fallbacks.      |
| 8 | **Locale-independent**| Uses **widget Keys** for app UI (`Key('signInButton')`), not display text.  |
| 9 | **Failure-aware**     | The case states what failure mode it would catch.                           |
| 10| **Cheap diagnosis**   | On failure, captures `screenshot` + relevant `read_logs` so triage doesn't need a re-run. |

**Rewrite signals** — if you see any of these, the test case is invalid, not the code:

- "Test passes locally, fails in CI" with no further detail → criterion 3.
- Uses `await tester.pump(Duration(seconds: 5))` to wait for UI → criterion 7.
- Asserts `find.byType(Container)` count → criterion 4 (structure, not behaviour).
- Hardcodes `"Sign in"`, `"Settings"`, `"Allow"` → **criterion 8**, your test will break the moment the OS or app is in a different language. Use `Key('signInButton')`. For system dialogs, parametrise the localized string in the test plan and pass it in.
- Depends on a previous test having logged in → criterion 5.
- "It's just a smoke test" with no AC mapped → criterion 1.

## Requirement → Test Traceability

Before adding tests, build (or have the user supply) a 3-column matrix:

| AC ID | Acceptance criterion (one sentence)                              | Test case ID(s) |
|-------|------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------|
| AC-1  | A signed-in user sees their email on the home screen.            | TC-HOME-01      |
| AC-2  | Tapping "Sign out" returns to the login screen within 2s.        | TC-AUTH-07      |

Rules:
- Every AC needs **≥ 1** test case. Flag gaps explicitly.
- Every test case needs **exactly 1** AC. Multi-AC cases are too coarse.
- ACs without a clear "Then" observable through Patrol or MCP tools are **specification bugs** — flag them, don't paper over them.

## Test pyramid

```
                        ┌──────────────┐
                        │  smoke (5)   │  ← golden flow on real hardware
                        │              │     run on every build
                        └──────────────┘
                     ┌──────────────────┐
                     │ patrol (~30)     │  ← run_patrol_suite on hardware
                     │  per-feature     │     before release
                     └──────────────────┘
                ┌──────────────────────────┐
                │     widget (~hundreds)   │  ← flutter test — no MCP
                └──────────────────────────┘
            ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
            │       unit (~thousands)          │  ← flutter test — no MCP
```

## MCP loop patterns

content.text:
**55/55 tests pass. 33 MCP tools wired.** No archive needed — the existing clean architecture absorbed Patrol cleanly.

## What changed

### New abstractions (multi-framework, multi-stack ready)
- `Platform` enum (Android/iOS) — already there.
- `ProjectType` enum (Flutter/NativeAndroid/NativeIos/ReactNative/Web/Unknown).
- `TestFramework` enum (Patrol/Flutter/XCUITest/Espresso/Detox/Playwright/Unknown).
- `ProjectInfo` entity with detected type + applicable test frameworks + flavors + package id.
- `ProjectInspector` Protocol + `FlutterProjectInspector` impl + `CompositeProjectInspector` registry.
- `EnvironmentRepository` Protocol + `SystemEnvironmentRepository` impl ("doctor").
- `PatrolRepository` Protocol — superset of `TestRepository` with Patrol-specific list/run.

### New use cases (4)
- `CheckEnvironment` — preflight doctor.
- `InspectProject` — Flutter? Patrol? Native? RN?
- `ListPatrolTests` / `RunPatrolTest` / `RunPatrolSuite`.

### Composite test routing
`CompositeTestRepository` now consults the `ProjectInspector` first: if `TestFramework.PATROL` is in the project's framework list, integration tests route through the Patrol runner. Otherwise they fall back to plain `flutter test`. Same use case calls. Adding XCUITest / Espresso / Detox is **3 lines in the container** + one new repository implementation.

### Tools (33 total, 5 new)
- `check_environment` — DOCTOR. Run first.
- `inspect_project` — what is this project?
- `list_patrol_tests` — discover tests.
- `run_patrol_test` — run one Patrol test.
- `run_patrol_suite` — run a whole directory.

Raw UI tools (`tap_text` etc.) are **kept** but their descriptions now explicitly say "system UI only — for app UI prefer Patrol." Claude will pick the right tool by reading the schema.

### Skill rewritten
`SKILL.md` now opens with `check_environment` + `inspect_project` as mandatory preflight, has a framework decision tree, and Rubric criterion 8 ("locale-independent") gives Claude a hard rule: **never hardcode display text for app UI — use Keys.** That single rule eliminates the Polish-language failures.

## Restart the MCP and try it

```bash
# in a fresh terminal:
cd /Users/michalgiza/Desktop/mcp_phone_controll && source .venv/bin/activate
.venv/bin/pytest                    # 55 tests in <0.1s, no device needed
```

Then in Claude Code: end this conversation (or `/mcp` → Reconnect `phone-controll`) so the new code is loaded. In a new session, paste:

```
You have an MCP called phone-controll. Run a preflight:
  1. check_environment — show me each item; halt on any red.
  2. list_devices and select my Android.
  3. inspect_project for /path/to/my/flutter/project — tell me whether
     Patrol is available.
  4. If Patrol is available, list_patrol_tests for that project.
  5. Pick one test and run_patrol_test.
  6. Report TestRun + screenshot + artifacts dir.
```

That run will exercise the entire new stack. When you're ready to add native iOS / RN / web later, the README's "Adding a new test framework" section is a four-step recipe — no architectural rework.

## Issue 1: `list_devices` shouldn't die when iOS toolchain is absent

content.text:
55/55 pass. The venv has `pymobiledevice3` at `.venv/bin/pymobiledevice3` — and our `_default_pymobiledevice3_path()` resolves to exactly that via `Path(sys.executable).parent`. So once Claude Code reloads the MCP, `list_devices` will:

1. Find pymobiledevice3 inside the venv (no PATH inheritance needed).
2. Even if it didn't, `process_runner` catches `FileNotFoundError` and returns a typed failure.
3. Even if iOS raised any exception, the composite now swallows it and Android still wins.

Three layers of defence. The only thing your running MCP is missing is **the new code** — it's the same long-lived process it spawned earlier, before these patches.

## Reload the MCP

Either of these:

```bash
# Option A: full restart
# Just close this Claude Code conversation and start a new one in the same dir.

# Option B: reconnect the server in place
# In your input box: type /mcp, pick phone-controll, choose Reconnect/Restart
```

After reload, ask Claude:

```
Use phone-controll.check_environment first. Then list_devices.
Confirm both Android and iOS surfaces work or report which fails.
```

`check_environment` will tell you each binary's status with a fix command if anything's still red — that's the surface that didn't exist when you first registered the MCP.

## Issue 2: Gradle fix in your app repo

You're absolutely right — that's a real bug, not test scaffolding. Two things:

- **Commit it in your app's repo, not here.** That fix lives in your Flutter project's `android/` config; it has nothing to do with this MCP. Open a PR there with a short title like `fix(android): unbreak flutter build apk`.
- **A `check_environment`-style preflight in your app's CI** would have caught it. Worth a follow-up: add a `flutter doctor && cd android && ./gradlew tasks` step early in CI so this class of bug never reaches a test session again.

## Going deeper than the welcome screen

Everything Claude listed (ATT/notification permission, UMP consent, URL paste, mic recording, album scan, diagnostics long-press, ad pipeline) — **that's exactly Patrol territory now.** Hardcoded `tap_text("Get Started")` would break the moment you ship Polish strings. The right shape:

```dart
// integration_test/onboarding_test.dart
void main() {
  patrolTest('full onboarding to home', ($) async {
    await $.pumpWidgetAndSettle(const MyApp());

    await $(#getStartedCta).tap();                          // Key('getStartedCta')
    await $.native.grantPermissionWhenInUse();              // notification/mic/loc
    await $(#umpAcceptAll).tap();
    await $(#urlPasteField).enterText('https://...');
    await $(#startScanButton).tap();
    await $(#scanComplete).waitUntilVisible(timeout: Duration(seconds: 30));
    expect($(#diagnosticsBanner), findsOneWidget);
  });
}
```
